EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of September 9, 2014 a line of thunderstorms moved though eastern Nebraska into Iowa and northern Missouri. Along the southwestern edge of the line a strong supercell formed, producing widespread wind and hail across far northwestern Missouri. This supercell went on to produce 5 brief and weak tornadoes across northwestern Missouri before finally dissipating. The main line of convection then caused wind damage across northern Missouri through the remainder of the overnight hours. Another tornado then formed early in the morning on September 10 near Kirksville, Missouri. This tornado moved into the town and caused some minor damage to trees and residences.  Once the wind and tornado threat decreased widespread heavy rain, producing up to 10 inches in some areas, caused flooding and flash flooding across much of northern Missouri, including closures to several main highways. No injuries were reported as a result of the severe weather and flooding, but the far northern part of the state received much damage on account of the winds and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light rain behind a departing thunderstorm complex allowed strong low level winds to reach the surface near Prairie Village. No widespread damage occurred, however a large tree fell onto a car in Prairie Village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm dumped anywhere from 6 to as much as 14 inches of snow across much of eastern Kansas.  The bulk of the snow fell during February 4th spreading from the southwest to the northeast during the pre-dawn hours of February 4th.  The snow was steady and became heavy at times.  The snow ended from west to east after sunset on the 4th and lingered just past midnight on the 5th across extreme eastern Kansas.  The heaviest snow band fell roughly along I-70 from Salina east through Manhattan through Topeka and into west Kansas City.  Many of those areas received 10-14 inches of snow.  The 24 hour snow totals ranked in the top 5 in many of the cities mentioned above.  Impacts were felt in travel especially late on February 4th as strong north winds caused blowing and drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lyon County received 5 to 7 inches of snow with the heaviest across the northern half of the county.  Neosho River measured 6.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms erupted around midnight south of Interstate 70 and moved southeast overnight. Baseball size hail broke windows in Sherman County, while extremely heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding across Logan and Gove counties. The Smoky Hill River and Hackberry Creek overflowed their banks resulting in minor flooding.  A tornado developed around 6 am CDT south of Gove and produced significant damage to a farmstead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Time estimated from Doppler radar. Law enforcement reported six-inch diameter tree limbs down. Relayed through emergency manager.

Precipitation was well above normal during the month of June at almost all cooperative observer sites in northwest Kansas. This resulted in improvement in the drought rating in some counties. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Decatur County improved from a Severe (D2) to Extreme (D3) rating at the beginning of June to a Moderate (D1) rating by month's end. Rainfall was 2.75 above normal at Oberlin and Norcatur, and 2.4 above normal at Dresden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early mornings hours of the 18th a mid level warm front set up over the area. High amounts of moisture advected into that warm front and steep mid level lapse rates led to a very unstable air mass. The difference in wind speed and direction also amounted to enough shear for rotating updrafts. The severe storms produced hail up to the size of tennis balls. Since the front didn't move much all morning storms developed and moved over the same areas causing isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail lasted about 15 mins and caused damage to vehicles, house siding, roof, and crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm dumped anywhere from 6 to as much as 14 inches of snow across much of eastern Kansas.  The bulk of the snow fell during February 4th spreading from the southwest to the northeast during the pre-dawn hours of February 4th.  The snow was steady and became heavy at times.  The snow ended from west to east after sunset on the 4th and lingered just past midnight on the 5th across extreme eastern Kansas.  The heaviest snow band fell roughly along I-70 from Salina east through Manhattan through Topeka and into west Kansas City.  Many of those areas received 10-14 inches of snow.  The 24 hour snow totals ranked in the top 5 in many of the cities mentioned above.  Impacts were felt in travel especially late on February 4th as strong north winds caused blowing and drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: White City observer measured 8 inches.  CoCoRaHs observer 2 miles east of Herington measured 10.5 inches in Morris County.

The heaviest snow band fell roughly along I-70 from Salina east through Manhattan through Topeka and into west Kansas City.  Many of those areas received 10-14 inches of snow.  The 24 hour snow totals ranked in the top 5 in many of the cities mentioned above.  Impacts were felt in travel especially late on February 4th as strong north winds caused blowing and drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer in Wamego measured 9.5 inches.  Much of the county received 9 to 12 inches of snow from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of July 7th a very unstable air mass was in place as a line of thunderstorms formed in southern Nebraska. These storms were responsible for 70 to 80 mph winds across a large portion of northeast Kansas. Many trees and power lines fell down in several local towns and cities. In some cases these large trees damaged homes and vehicles.

The first snow of the fall developed and moved across the area.  Most areas received 1 to 3 inches.  However, a narrow area of enhanced snow developed across Stanton county.  Most of Stanton county had 5 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5.5 inches was reported at a location 4 miles NNE of Big Bow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of storms brought localized heavy rains to the eastern Kansas on the 9th leading to isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closed at 294th Street and Edwards Road due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of storms brought localized heavy rains to the eastern Kansas on the 9th leading to isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sedgwick Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early mornings hours of the 18th a mid level warm front set up over the area. High amounts of moisture advected into that warm front and steep mid level lapse rates led to a very unstable air mass. The difference in wind speed and direction also amounted to enough shear for rotating updrafts. The severe storms produced hail up to the size of tennis balls. Since the front didn't move much all morning storms developed and moved over the same areas causing isolated flash flooding.

On the afternoon of the 20th a cold front moved through the area causing isolated thunderstorms to develop. The dew points ahead of the front were close to 70 degrees which allowed for decent instability to fuel the storms. As storms weakened severe downbursts damaged trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of power lines broken and several leaning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were down and an empty semi trailer blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 17th a complex of thunderstorms developed over northern Nebraska and started moving south. Over time a strong cold pool developed and drove the storms into north central Kansas by the evening. These storms caused severe wind damage to certain locations before the nocturnal inversion began preventing the winds from reaching the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of power lines down and medium sized trees uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of the 3rd elevated thunderstorms developed on the nose of a low level jet. Once formed the storms were slow to move to the east. Additional developed allow for areas to receive large amounts of rain in a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rescue operations for a motorist near the intersection of Seward and Golden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This wind event was due to an Alberta Clipper, diving from North Dakota into the Great Lakes. The associated cold front moved through late this Sunday afternoon, and the pressure gradient was tight between the 990 mb low and the 1040 mb high over Alberta and Saskatchewan. Ahead of the front, the pressure gradient was weak. With the rapidly rising pressure and the burst of deeper mixing during peak heating, the increase in winds was dramatic and even sudden at some locations. A squall was even reported at some automated observing stations. This was a tremendously cold, dense air mass enveloping the region. High temperatures on this date were in the 50s, but the following day, highs were only in the teens. Peak wind gusts of 45 to 57 mph were widespread across North Central Kansas. The maximum gust measured was 60 mph at the Beloit airport. Sustained winds even exceeded 40 mph, in some locations, but only for a brief time. The initial burst of winds produced blowing dust over portions of North Central Kansas, reducing the visibility to below 3 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust was measured by the AWOS at the Beloit Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm moved southeast out of Nebraska and produced one strong tornado in northeast Nemaha county.  The supercell storm continued to track southeast and produced another tornado in Brown County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down around the intersection of highway 71 and 63 around 1030 pm CDT.  The damage path moved southeast and included several homes that were severely damaged and one totally destroyed.  The worst damage occurred to a slab home anchored to the foundation by anchor bolts installed with nuts and washers every 12-18 inches.  All exterior and interior walls were destroyed however the debris was primarily laid on top of the slab with some debris blown to the south.  Two adult residents took shelter in a tub and survived with minor injuries although the tub was gone and it was suspected to have been blown into a lake to the south.  The tornado damage path continued southeast for approximately 6.5 to 7.5 miles total.  EF3 rating with winds to around 140 mph.  The end time of the tornado was around 1043 pm CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primarily between 9 p.m. on Wednesday the 17th and 5 a.m. on Thursday the 18th, Jewell and Mitchell counties received a broad coating of 2 to 3.5��� of snow, with lesser totals closer to 1 reported in eastern portions of Smith and Osborne counties. Per a combination of NWS cooperative observers, CoCoRaHs observers and trained spotters, a few of the highest overnight totals featured 3.5��� at Lovewell Dam, 3.3��� at Jewell and Simpson, 3.0��� two miles north-northwest of Hunter and 2.6��� at Mankato. Fortunately, light wind made this an almost ���ideal���, relatively low-impact snowfall, as sustained easterly speeds only averaged around 5 MPH. ||From a broader geographical perspective, the local North Central Kansas area resided along the northwestern fringes of a larger snowfall swath of generally 2-5��� that mainly affected central and northeast portions of the Sunflower State. Between 9 p.m. and midnight on the 17th, steady snow spread into the local area from the south, before very slowly ending from west-to-east during the pre-dawn hours. On the large scale of the mid and upper levels, this fairly low-impact event was driven primarily by broad isentropic lift focused along and north of a low-amplitude shortwave trough shifting across KS and toward the MO/IA border through the night, with only a fairly weak contribution from frontogenetic forcing. At the surface, the very light wind was fostered by a weak pressure gradient on the southwest fringes of a broad high centered over the Upper Mississippi Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative observers measured 3.5 inches at Lovewell Dam, 3.3 inches at Jewell, 2.6 inches in Mankato and 2.4 inches in Burr Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient caused strong gusty winds across southeast Kansas and resulted in at least one grass fire to rapidly spread and become out of control.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty winds over 50 mph led to a rapidly spreading grass fire near Galena. The fire was started by a fallen power line that had been blown down due to the winds. The fire burned approximately 500 acres and destroyed at least three outdoor structures and one car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level shortwave moved east out of the Central Rockies in into the Central Plains Wednesday afternoon into Thursday morning, December 17th and 18th. As this shortwave approached, an area of low pressure developed and slowly deepened across the Texas Panhandle as the day progressed. This increased moisture advection into central and western Kansas with an east to southeast flow across southwest Kansas. Lift increased in the mid-levels in the afternoon with precipitation starting across the KS/OK border around 3 PM. This area of precipitation spread northeast into the early evening hours. Precipitation initially started out as rain or a mix of rain and sleet, then quickly transitioned over to snow by sunset. A band of moderate to heavy snow stretched from Clark to Stafford counties and continued to train over this same area as the evening progressed. This band slowly dissipated by late evening. Scattered snow showers were observed across this same area and farther north of this first band around midnight leading to additional snowfall accumulations. Snow showers then tapered off from west to east by 4AM Thursday morning. By the time the snow ended, 2+ inches fell between the lines from Clark State Lake in Clark County to Shaffer in Rush County and from Buttermilk in Comanche County to Neola in Stafford County. Parts of northeastern Ford County also received over 2 inches of snow. The highest snowfall amount reported was 6 inches around Belpre in Edwards County. The remainder of Ford, Gray, and Hodgeman counties received 1-2 inches with the other surrounding counties receiving a trace to 1 inch.

During the late afternoon through the early morning on the following day, a band of moderate to heavy snowfall setup along the Kansas-Nebraska border, stretching west into the foothills of the Rockies. The band intensified during the evening and lasted through almost sunrise before dissipating. There was almost no wind with this snowfall, which is rare for this area. Total snowfall accumulations for Northwest Kansas ranged from 11.5 at Atwood to 3 near Norcatur and Jennings north of Highway 24, with 2 or less south of the highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 9 9 N of Atwood to 11.5 in Atwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, January 2014 marked the 20th consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued at least some portion of this six county North Central Kansas area, prolonging one of the most notable droughts in decades. January was the second consecutive month of solidly below normal precipitation area-wide, and according to data from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, roughly 60 percent of this area received no more than half of normal January precipitation (based on long-term January normals generally ranging between 0.40 and 0.65). As a result, there was a one-category degradation in drought classification across most of the area during the month. Category D2 Severe Drought expanded eastward from western Phillips and Rooks counties, to now include all of Phillips, Rooks, Smith and Osborne counties, along with western Jewell and Mitchell. Meanwhile, eastern portions of Jewell and Mitchell counties degraded to D1 Moderate Drought from D0 Abnormally Dry (considered to be one category better than true drought). Due to these January changes, North Central Kansas now contained the greatest areal coverage of D2-or-worse Drought since mid-summer 2013.||For most of the six-county area, the majority of January precipitation fell in the form of 3-6 of fairly dry snow, thus providing very little liquid moisture equivalent. Also for most of the area, the brunt of January precipitation fell on both the first and last days of the month, with very little in between. Thanks to not only a dry December and January, but also a predominantly dry November, much of North Central Kansas recorded one of the Top-5 driest 86-day stretches on record between the dates of Nov. 6-Jan. 30, including at Beloit (0.32) and two miles southwest of Alton (0.23). Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the leanest January precipitation totals included 0.07 at Hunter, 0.12 at Cawker City, 0.14 at Jewell and 0.17 at Logan. Although still well below normal, a few of the highest monthly amounts occurred at stations located in Mankato (0.36), Smith Center (0.35) and Lebanon (0.34). According to the USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of January, state-averaged soil moisture supplies clearly declined during the month. Topsoil moisture degraded notably since late-December, as the short to very short category increased from 29% to 57%, and the adequate category dropped to 42%. Subsoil moisture also degraded during the month, increasing from 39% to 57% short to very short, while the adequate category decreased to 43%.

Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the leanest January precipitation totals included 0.07 at Hunter, 0.12 at Cawker City, 0.14 at Jewell and 0.17 at Logan. Although still well below normal, a few of the highest monthly amounts occurred at stations located in Mankato (0.36), Smith Center (0.35) and Lebanon (0.34). According to the USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of January, state-averaged soil moisture supplies clearly declined during the month. Topsoil moisture degraded notably since late-December, as the short to very short category increased from 29% to 57%, and the adequate category dropped to 42%. Subsoil moisture also degraded during the month, increasing from 39% to 57% short to very short, while the adequate category decreased to 43%.

A weak upper level shortwave moved east out of the Central Rockies in into the Central Plains Wednesday afternoon into Thursday morning, December 17th and 18th. As this shortwave approached, an area of low pressure developed and slowly deepened across the Texas Panhandle as the day progressed. This increased moisture advection into central and western Kansas with an east to southeast flow across southwest Kansas. Lift increased in the mid-levels in the afternoon with precipitation starting across the KS/OK border around 3 PM. This area of precipitation spread northeast into the early evening hours. Precipitation initially started out as rain or a mix of rain and sleet, then quickly transitioned over to snow by sunset. A band of moderate to heavy snow stretched from Clark to Stafford counties and continued to train over this same area as the evening progressed. This band slowly dissipated by late evening. Scattered snow showers were observed across this same area and farther north of this first band around midnight leading to additional snowfall accumulations. Snow showers then tapered off from west to east by 4AM Thursday morning. By the time the snow ended, 2+ inches fell between the lines from Clark State Lake in Clark County to Shaffer in Rush County and from Buttermilk in Comanche County to Neola in Stafford County. Parts of northeastern Ford County also received over 2 inches of snow. The highest snowfall amount reported was 6 inches around Belpre in Edwards County. The remainder of Ford, Gray, and Hodgeman counties received 1-2 inches with the other surrounding counties receiving a trace to 1 inch.

During the late afternoon through the early morning on the following day, a band of moderate to heavy snowfall setup along the Kansas-Nebraska border, stretching west into the foothills of the Rockies. The band intensified during the evening and lasted through almost sunrise before dissipating. There was almost no wind with this snowfall, which is rare for this area. Total snowfall accumulations for Northwest Kansas ranged from 11.5 at Atwood to 3 near Norcatur and Jennings north of Highway 24, with 2 or less south of the highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 11 7 N of St. Francis to 6.5 in St. Francis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of sleet and snow fell across Northeast Kansas beginning late on Saturday March 1st and ending on Sunday March 2nd across the area.  Some of the counties received between one half and one inch of sleet and one to four inches of snow through the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of one inch of sleet along with 2 to 4 inches of snow were received from across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm dumped anywhere from 6 to as much as 14 inches of snow across much of eastern Kansas.  The bulk of the snow fell during February 4th spreading from the southwest to the northeast during the pre-dawn hours of February 4th.  The snow was steady and became heavy at times.  The snow ended from west to east after sunset on the 4th and lingered just past midnight on the 5th across extreme eastern Kansas.  The heaviest snow band fell roughly along I-70 from Salina east through Manhattan through Topeka and into west Kansas City.  Many of those areas received 10-14 inches of snow.  The 24 hour snow totals ranked in the top 5 in many of the cities mentioned above.  Impacts were felt in travel especially late on February 4th as strong north winds caused blowing and drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 6 to 8 inches were common across Coffey County from this storm. Gridley received 7.1 inches from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning through the afternoon high winds were reported across most of northwest Kansas.  Wind gust reports of 58-60 MPH were very common across the area.  The strongest measured gust of 64 MPH was reported at the Goodland airport.  The high winds also contributed to wide spread blowing dust which reduced visibilities down to less than a quarter mile a few times through the day.  The poor visibilities contributed to three accidents on Highway 83 near Rexford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated from nearby mesonet and ASOS/AWOS stations in neighboring counties.

During the overnight and through the morning hours the arctic air mass over the area combined with a recent snow pack, caused temperatures to fall into the teens below zero. Temperatures warmed during the afternoon causing wind chills to increase to between -15 and 0.  After sunset on the 5th wind chills around -26 returned to parts of Northwest Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few hours after midnight a strong cold front moved south through the area from Nebraska. Northerly winds immediately behind the front gusted to almost 70 MPH for a short time.  The high winds reduced visibilities to around half a mile for a brief time.  The peak gust reported was 67 MPH near Colby, with the other high wind reports being just under 60 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported at the Oakley airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm dumped anywhere from 6 to as much as 14 inches of snow across much of eastern Kansas.  The bulk of the snow fell during February 4th spreading from the southwest to the northeast during the pre-dawn hours of February 4th.  The snow was steady and became heavy at times.  The snow ended from west to east after sunset on the 4th and lingered just past midnight on the 5th across extreme eastern Kansas.  The heaviest snow band fell roughly along I-70 from Salina east through Manhattan through Topeka and into west Kansas City.  Many of those areas received 10-14 inches of snow.  The 24 hour snow totals ranked in the top 5 in many of the cities mentioned above.  Impacts were felt in travel especially late on February 4th as strong north winds caused blowing and drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 8 to 12 inches were common across Dickinson County from this storm. Enterprise received 11.8 inches from this storm where Herington measured 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal precipitation during the month helped improve the drought status for Northwest Kansas.  Many COOP sites reported above normal precipitation for the month.  However the D2 category drought persisted, but did decreased in area to include counties east from Sherman to Thomas, and south from Sherman to Greeley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Category D2 severe drought continued across the county.  The monthly precipitation was 0.35 above normal for Tribune 1W.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms fired across portions of South Central Kansas in which one rare tornado touched down in Harper county causing minimal damage to trees and round hay bales.
Storms fired across portions of South Central Kansas in which one rare tornado touched down in Harper county causing minimal damage to trees and round hay bales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado moved to the north causing minor damage to trees and round hay bales.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level shortwave moved east out of the Central Rockies in into the Central Plains Wednesday afternoon into Thursday morning, December 17th and 18th. As this shortwave approached, an area of low pressure developed and slowly deepened across the Texas Panhandle as the day progressed. This increased moisture advection into central and western Kansas with an east to southeast flow across southwest Kansas. Lift increased in the mid-levels in the afternoon with precipitation starting across the KS/OK border around 3 PM. This area of precipitation spread northeast into the early evening hours. Precipitation initially started out as rain or a mix of rain and sleet, then quickly transitioned over to snow by sunset. A band of moderate to heavy snow stretched from Clark to Stafford counties and continued to train over this same area as the evening progressed. This band slowly dissipated by late evening. Scattered snow showers were observed across this same area and farther north of this first band around midnight leading to additional snowfall accumulations. Snow showers then tapered off from west to east by 4AM Thursday morning. By the time the snow ended, 2+ inches fell between the lines from Clark State Lake in Clark County to Shaffer in Rush County and from Buttermilk in Comanche County to Neola in Stafford County. Parts of northeastern Ford County also received over 2 inches of snow. The highest snowfall amount reported was 6 inches around Belpre in Edwards County. The remainder of Ford, Gray, and Hodgeman counties received 1-2 inches with the other surrounding counties receiving a trace to 1 inch.

During the afternoon an arctic front moved south through Nebraska into Kansas.  As the front approached Decatur and Graham counties wind gusts strengthen, with a peak gust of 60 MPH recorded at the Oberlin Airport and 58 MPH reported at Hill City Airport. The high winds caused widespread visibility reduction to around two miles across Northwest Kansas.

A weak upper level shortwave moved across the Central Rockies and through the Central Plains Monday into Tuesday afternoon, December 29th and 30th. Low and Mid level moisture increased as this feature approached with well below normal temperatures observed at the surface. Light snow started to fall Monday afternoon across west central Kansas and spread eastward into the evening. Light snow continued overnight before tapering off shortly after sunrise Tuesday. Additional snow showers then developed late morning into the afternoon before completely ending by 5PM Tuesday. By the time the snow ended, 2+ inches of snow was reported across most of the area with portions of Scott, Lane, Finney, Gray, and Haskell counties receiving over 4 inches of snow. Another area that received over 4 inches of snow was across portions of south central Kansas.

Drought conditions continued across all of northwest Kansas during January. The area is rated as being in D2 (Severe) to D3 (Extreme) drought. Precipitation during the month was generally below normal although some higher amounts in Cheyenne county were above normal where observers reported three quarters of an inch of moisture.  While amounts ranged from 0.10 to 0.80 inches, most totals were only 0.20 to 0.30 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cheyenne county is rated as being in D3 (Extreme) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Northwest Kansas during the morning.  Behind the front winds gusted around 60 MPH.  The high winds continued until the early afternoon then began to decline.  The peak gust was 66 MPH reported 4 N of Atwood.  The high winds created widespread blowing dust across Northwest Kansas, with the worst of the blowing dust over Rawlins, Sheridan, and Decatur counties where visibilities dropped to near zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thomas county met high wind criteria based on Sherman, Rawlins, and Sheridan county all reporting high wind gusts.

Precipitation ranged from a few hundredths of an inch to around a third of an inch, which was an inch to an inch and a half below normal for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheridan county is rated in D2 (Severe) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm tracked from southern Kansas into Maine on February 4-5, 2014.  Some of the highest snow totals with this system fell from northeast Kansas into northern Missouri where some areas exceeded a foot of snow.  Northerly winds on the back side of this system gusted up to 30 mph and produced substantial blowing and drifting.  Many areas reported drifts of 2 to 3 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm trekked through Kansas and Missouri on February 4 and 5. By the time the storm finished it dropped around 6-10 of snow across the entire area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved into southwest and south central Kansas the night of Monday, February 3rd, and increased in intensity during the day Tuesday, February 4th. There was snow, moderate to heavy at times, especially across south central Kansas Tuesday afternoon.  Before the storm ended late Tuesday night the 4th, there was a small area of 11-inch snowfall covering southern Pratt and northern Barber Counties.  There was a pocket of 8 to 10 inches of snow from northeast Edwards County to northwest Kiowa County, as well as a 10-inch band of snow from northern Meade into eastern Seward Counties.  Yet another small area of 8 inch snowfall fell in southwestern Barber County.  Outside of the heavier snowfall, from 4 to 8 inches of snow fell southeast of line from just west of Liberal to just north of Garden City to west of Hays.  Lesser amounts of 2 to 3 inches of snow fell just northwest of that line, from Hugoton, to near Lakin to Ness City to near Wakeeney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7.5 inches was reported 7 miles west of Hardtner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, February 10th, an upper level short wave moved from Nevada to the central Rockies.  This upper level disturbance then crossed the|central high plains late Tuesday, approaching a mid-level frontogenesis, boundary along the 700mb baroclinic zone. The mid-level baroclinic zone remained nearly stationary across southern sections of southwest and south central Kansas into early Wednesday evening.  Some snow started early on Tuesday afternoon across northeastern Finney County, where a small area of 5 to 7 inches of snow fell.  Another area of 5 to 6 inches of snow fell across southwest and northwest Kiowa County.  Then, a narrow band of heavier snow formed along the mid-level baroclinic zone overnight Tuesday night, and dropped a 20 to 35 mile wide band of snow from 5.5 inches to 9.6 inches.  This band was from far western Haskell County to near Meade in Meade County to near Ashland in Clark County, to south of Buttermilk in southern Comanche County.  The highest snowfall amount reported was along the Kansas/Oklahoma state line, south of Buttermilk.  There was a narrow area surrounding this heavier band where 2 to 4 inches of snow fell, south of a line from northeastern Grant County to southwestern Gray County to near Clark County State Lake, to north of Lewis in Edwards County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7.0 inches was reported 12 miles NNW of Kalvesta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level shortwave moved across the Central Rockies and through the Central Plains Monday into Tuesday afternoon, December 29th and 30th. Low and Mid level moisture increased as this feature approached with well below normal temperatures observed at the surface. Light snow started to fall Monday afternoon across west central Kansas and spread eastward into the evening. Light snow continued overnight before tapering off shortly after sunrise Tuesday. Additional snow showers then developed late morning into the afternoon before completely ending by 5PM Tuesday. By the time the snow ended, 2+ inches of snow was reported across most of the area with portions of Scott, Lane, Finney, Gray, and Haskell counties receiving over 4 inches of snow. Another area that received over 4 inches of snow was across portions of south central Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level shortwave moved east out of the Central Rockies in into the Central Plains Wednesday afternoon into Thursday morning, December 17th and 18th. As this shortwave approached, an area of low pressure developed and slowly deepened across the Texas Panhandle as the day progressed. This increased moisture advection into central and western Kansas with an east to southeast flow across southwest Kansas. Lift increased in the mid-levels in the afternoon with precipitation starting across the KS/OK border around 3 PM. This area of precipitation spread northeast into the early evening hours. Precipitation initially started out as rain or a mix of rain and sleet, then quickly transitioned over to snow by sunset. A band of moderate to heavy snow stretched from Clark to Stafford counties and continued to train over this same area as the evening progressed. This band slowly dissipated by late evening. Scattered snow showers were observed across this same area and farther north of this first band around midnight leading to additional snowfall accumulations. Snow showers then tapered off from west to east by 4AM Thursday morning. By the time the snow ended, 2+ inches fell between the lines from Clark State Lake in Clark County to Shaffer in Rush County and from Buttermilk in Comanche County to Neola in Stafford County. Parts of northeastern Ford County also received over 2 inches of snow. The highest snowfall amount reported was 6 inches around Belpre in Edwards County. The remainder of Ford, Gray, and Hodgeman counties received 1-2 inches with the other surrounding counties receiving a trace to 1 inch.

Scattered non-severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening hours. One storm was strong enough to produce penny size hail.

A strong upper-level storm system tracked through Upper Midwest during the day of March 27th, trailing a sharp cold front southward through Missouri and eastern Kansas. Severe thunderstorms with large hail and damaging winds developed in extreme east central Kansas and tracked across portions of the Kansas City metropolitan area. Tornadic thunderstorms developed northeast of Kansas causing damage to a number of homes in north central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near 151st and Antioch Road.

During the afternoon to evening hours on March 1st light freezing rain and sleet fell across portions of eastern Kansas. While there was not necessarily a period of incredible accumulation amounts the long duration of the event allowed for several areas to receive more than 1/2 of sleet, and around 3 to 5 inches of snow. The storm lasted overnight with several rounds of wintry precipitation occurring, and eventually moved out of the area around 2 pm on March 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters across the area reported about a half inch to an inch of sleet, occurring mostly during the evening hours on March 1st through the overnight hours on March 2nd. Aside from the sleet accumulations snowfall approaching 3 to 6 inches also accumulated through the overnight hours on March 1st into March 2nd. The long-duration event ended during the afternoon hours on March 2nd.

Aside from the sleet accumulations snowfall approaching 3 to 6 inches also accumulated through the overnight hours on March 1st into March 2nd. The long-duration event ended during the afternoon hours on March 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Northwest Kansas during the morning.  Behind the front winds gusted around 60 MPH. The high winds created widespread blowing dust across Northwest Kansas, with the worst of the blowing dust over Rawlins, Sheridan, and Decatur counties where visibilities dropped to near zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cheyenne county met high wind criteria based on the high winds reported in Rawlins, Sherman, and Yuma counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level shortwave moved east out of the Central Rockies in into the Central Plains Wednesday afternoon into Thursday morning, December 17th and 18th. As this shortwave approached, an area of low pressure developed and slowly deepened across the Texas Panhandle as the day progressed. This increased moisture advection into central and western Kansas with an east to southeast flow across southwest Kansas. Lift increased in the mid-levels in the afternoon with precipitation starting across the KS/OK border around 3 PM. This area of precipitation spread northeast into the early evening hours. Precipitation initially started out as rain or a mix of rain and sleet, then quickly transitioned over to snow by sunset. A band of moderate to heavy snow stretched from Clark to Stafford counties and continued to train over this same area as the evening progressed. This band slowly dissipated by late evening. Scattered snow showers were observed across this same area and farther north of this first band around midnight leading to additional snowfall accumulations. Snow showers then tapered off from west to east by 4AM Thursday morning. By the time the snow ended, 2+ inches fell between the lines from Clark State Lake in Clark County to Shaffer in Rush County and from Buttermilk in Comanche County to Neola in Stafford County. Parts of northeastern Ford County also received over 2 inches of snow. The highest snowfall amount reported was 6 inches around Belpre in Edwards County. The remainder of Ford, Gray, and Hodgeman counties received 1-2 inches with the other surrounding counties receiving a trace to 1 inch.
On the late evening of June 11th isolated thunderstorms produced severe winds in north central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs up to 6 inches in diameter down in the Clifton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved into southwest and south central Kansas the night of Monday, February 3rd, and increased in intensity during the day Tuesday, February 4th. There was snow, moderate to heavy at times, especially across south central Kansas Tuesday afternoon.  Before the storm ended late Tuesday night the 4th, there was a small area of 11-inch snowfall covering southern Pratt and northern Barber Counties.  There was a pocket of 8 to 10 inches of snow from northeast Edwards County to northwest Kiowa County, as well as a 10-inch band of snow from northern Meade into eastern Seward Counties.  Yet another small area of 8 inch snowfall fell in southwestern Barber County.  Outside of the heavier snowfall, from 4 to 8 inches of snow fell southeast of line from just west of Liberal to just north of Garden City to west of Hays.  Lesser amounts of 2 to 3 inches of snow fell just northwest of that line, from Hugoton, to near Lakin to Ness City to near Wakeeney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 11 inches was reported at Sawyer.

Scattered snow showers were observed across this same area and farther north of this first band around midnight leading to additional snowfall accumulations. Snow showers then tapered off from west to east by 4AM Thursday morning. By the time the snow ended, 2+ inches fell between the lines from Clark State Lake in Clark County to Shaffer in Rush County and from Buttermilk in Comanche County to Neola in Stafford County. Parts of northeastern Ford County also received over 2 inches of snow. The highest snowfall amount reported was 6 inches around Belpre in Edwards County. The remainder of Ford, Gray, and Hodgeman counties received 1-2 inches with the other surrounding counties receiving a trace to 1 inch.

A weak upper level shortwave moved across the Central Rockies and through the Central Plains Monday into Tuesday afternoon, December 29th and 30th. Low and Mid level moisture increased as this feature approached with well below normal temperatures observed at the surface. Light snow started to fall Monday afternoon across west central Kansas and spread eastward into the evening. Light snow continued overnight before tapering off shortly after sunrise Tuesday. Additional snow showers then developed late morning into the afternoon before completely ending by 5PM Tuesday. By the time the snow ended, 2+ inches of snow was reported across most of the area with portions of Scott, Lane, Finney, Gray, and Haskell counties receiving over 4 inches of snow. Another area that received over 4 inches of snow was across portions of south central Kansas.

As this shortwave approached, an area of low pressure developed and slowly deepened across the Texas Panhandle as the day progressed. This increased moisture advection into central and western Kansas with an east to southeast flow across southwest Kansas. Lift increased in the mid-levels in the afternoon with precipitation starting across the KS/OK border around 3 PM. This area of precipitation spread northeast into the early evening hours. During the afternoon an arctic front moved south through Nebraska into Kansas.  As the front approached Decatur and Graham counties wind gusts strengthen, with a peak gust of 60 MPH recorded at the Oberlin Airport and 58 MPH reported at Hill City Airport. The high winds caused widespread visibility reduction to around two miles across Northwest Kansas.

A potent winter storm moved into southwest and south central Kansas the night of Monday, February 3rd, and increased in intensity during the day Tuesday, February 4th. There was snow, moderate to heavy at times, especially across south central Kansas Tuesday afternoon.  Before the storm ended late Tuesday night the 4th, there was a small area of 11-inch snowfall covering southern Pratt and northern Barber Counties.  There was a pocket of 8 to 10 inches of snow from northeast Edwards County to northwest Kiowa County, as well as a 10-inch band of snow from northern Meade into eastern Seward Counties.  Yet another small area of 8 inch snowfall fell in southwestern Barber County.  Outside of the heavier snowfall, from 4 to 8 inches of snow fell southeast of line from just west of Liberal to just north of Garden City to west of Hays.  Lesser amounts of 2 to 3 inches of snow fell just northwest of that line, from Hugoton, to near Lakin to Ness City to near Wakeeney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6.0 inches was reported at a location 4 miles ESE of Kingsdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm tracked from southern Kansas into Maine on February 4-5, 2014.  Some of the highest snow totals with this system fell from northeast Kansas into northern Missouri where some areas exceeded a foot of snow.  Northerly winds on the back side of this system gusted up to 30 mph and produced substantial blowing and drifting.  Many areas reported drifts of 2 to 3 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm trekked through Kansas and Missouri on February 4 and 5. By the time the storm finished it dropped around a foot of snow across the entire area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, February 10th, an upper level short wave moved from Nevada to the central Rockies.  This upper level disturbance then crossed the|central high plains late Tuesday, approaching a mid-level frontogenesis, boundary along the 700mb baroclinic zone. The mid-level baroclinic zone remained nearly stationary across southern sections of southwest and south central Kansas into early Wednesday evening.  Some snow started early on Tuesday afternoon across northeastern Finney County, where a small area of 5 to 7 inches of snow fell.  Another area of 5 to 6 inches of snow fell across southwest and northwest Kiowa County.  Then, a narrow band of heavier snow formed along the mid-level baroclinic zone overnight Tuesday night, and dropped a 20 to 35 mile wide band of snow from 5.5 inches to 9.6 inches.  This band was from far western Haskell County to near Meade in Meade County to near Ashland in Clark County, to south of Buttermilk in southern Comanche County.  The highest snowfall amount reported was along the Kansas/Oklahoma state line, south of Buttermilk.  There was a narrow area surrounding this heavier band where 2 to 4 inches of snow fell, south of a line from northeastern Grant County to southwestern Gray County to near Clark County State Lake, to north of Lewis in Edwards County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5.5 inches was reported 9 miles east of Kalvesta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slick driving conditions caused a fatal accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fatality accident occurred due to icy roads after a winter storm moved through the area. Two vehicles were parked beside a road of an exit ramp off I-35, with people standing between the cars. An 18-wheeler was exiting the interstate and struck the back of one of the vehicles. One of the people between the cars was caught between the cars and killed.

On the afternoon of April 2 a warm front was lifting into southern Kansas as mid level flow switched to the southwest. Warm moist air pushed northward up and over that front where steep mid level were in place. Elevated thunderstorms began to develop in this unstable environment. Large hail the size of quarters and golf balls were common with the storms that afternoon and evening. A few storms later that night were able to generate severe damaging winds down to the surface. This is very uncommon and these winds damaged several barns, trees, and power lines over a few counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covering the ground.

An arctic air mass dropped south across the plains on March 1st, with a mixture of freezing drizzle and sleet.  This led to hazardous driving conditions across Kansas on March the 1st and March the 2nd, 2014.  The wintry mixture of freezing drizzle and sleet eventually transitioned to all snow as a significant upper level trough moved across the Midwest.   Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches across the area, with some areas also receiving an inch of sleet.  The heaviest snowfall occurred along US Highway 50 from just south of Hutchinson to Newton to near Mount Hope, where almost 5 inches of snow fell.  Numerous auto accidents occurred from the mixture of freezing drizzle, sleet and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of snow fell across the area.  A half inch to an inch of sleet also fell creating slick streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system moved into the central United States, bringing several rounds of severe thunderstorms to the region.  Warm and moist air streamed northward out of the Gulf of Mexico on Saturday ahead of this system.  Strong thunderstorms quickly developed by mid-day and raced eastward across eastern Kansas and much of Missouri. Large hail up to the size of golf balls, damaging straight-line winds in excess of 60 mph, a brief tornado near Odessa, Missouri, and torrential rainfall moved through the area by mid-afternoon.||However, due to the speed and quick eastward progression of activity across the region Sunday afternoon, the atmosphere was able to recover to produce another round of severe thunderstorms across portions of eastern Kansas and western Missouri.  A second tornado occurred in the region near Ft. Scott, Kansas.  The formation and dissipation of the second tornado near Hume, MO was captured on live television by a Kansas City news station.  Minimal damage occurred as a result of the tornadoes, however isolated EF-2 damage occurred with the storm in east central Kansas. EF-0 damage was noted with the tornado near Odessa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass dropped south across the plains on March 1st, with a mixture of freezing drizzle and sleet.  This led to hazardous driving conditions across Kansas on March the 1st and March the 2nd, 2014.  The wintry mixture of freezing drizzle and sleet eventually transitioned to all snow as a significant upper level trough moved across the Midwest.   Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches across the area, with some areas also receiving an inch of sleet.  The heaviest snowfall occurred along US Highway 50 from just south of Hutchinson to Newton to near Mount Hope, where almost 5 inches of snow fell.  Numerous auto accidents occurred from the mixture of freezing drizzle, sleet and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system moved into the central United States, bringing several rounds of severe thunderstorms to the region.  Warm and moist air streamed northward out of the Gulf of Mexico on Saturday ahead of this system.  Strong thunderstorms quickly developed by mid-day and raced eastward across eastern Kansas and much of Missouri. Large hail up to the size of golf balls, damaging straight-line winds in excess of 60 mph, a brief tornado near Odessa, Missouri, and torrential rainfall moved through the area by mid-afternoon.||However, due to the speed and quick eastward progression of activity across the region Sunday afternoon, the atmosphere was able to recover to produce another round of severe thunderstorms across portions of eastern Kansas and western Missouri.  A second tornado occurred in the region near Ft. Scott, Kansas.  The formation and dissipation of the second tornado near Hume, MO was captured on live television by a Kansas City news station.  Minimal damage occurred as a result of the tornadoes, however isolated EF-2 damage occurred with the storm in east central Kansas. EF-0 damage was noted with the tornado near Odessa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado path is a continuation of the same tornado that came out of Bourbon County. The most significant damage occurred as the tornado strengthened just north of the Linn and Bourbon County line. In rural Linn County, between Pleasanton, Kansas and Prescott, Kansas an outdoor garage storing heavy equipment was completely destroyed and nearly removed from its foundation. At this same location an empty semi trailer was tossed was tossed over 200 yards into a grove of trees and several other smaller vehicles were tossed about the property. Near Pleasanton, Kansas a church that was built in the 1880's was completely destroyed. The path of the tornado ended just east or southeast of Pleasanton in an open field.

On Tuesday, February 10th, an upper level short wave moved from Nevada to the central Rockies.  This upper level disturbance then crossed the|central high plains late Tuesday, approaching a mid-level frontogenesis, boundary along the 700mb baroclinic zone. The mid-level baroclinic zone remained nearly stationary across southern sections of southwest and south central Kansas into early Wednesday evening.  Some snow started early on Tuesday afternoon across northeastern Finney County, where a small area of 5 to 7 inches of snow fell.  Another area of 5 to 6 inches of snow fell across southwest and northwest Kiowa County.  Then, a narrow band of heavier snow formed along the mid-level baroclinic zone overnight Tuesday night, and dropped a 20 to 35 mile wide band of snow from 5.5 inches to 9.6 inches.  This band was from far western Haskell County to near Meade in Meade County to near Ashland in Clark County, to south of Buttermilk in southern Comanche County.  The highest snowfall amount reported was along the Kansas/Oklahoma state line, south of Buttermilk.  There was a narrow area surrounding this heavier band where 2 to 4 inches of snow fell, south of a line from northeastern Grant County to southwestern Gray County to near Clark County State Lake, to north of Lewis in Edwards County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 inches was reported at a location 7 miles south of Centerview.

The very dry conditions continued during the month of April, with some areas falling 2 to 3 inches below normal for the month.  Coupled with a dry March, some areas of South Central Kansas, actually climbed into the extreme drought (D3) category by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unusually dry month of April, led to most of the county moving from a severe drought (D2) into an extreme drought (D3) by the middle of the month.

An arctic air mass dropped south across the plains on March 1st, with a mixture of freezing drizzle and sleet.  This led to hazardous driving conditions across Kansas on March the 1st and March the 2nd, 2014.  The wintry mixture of freezing drizzle and sleet eventually transitioned to all snow as a significant upper level trough moved across the Midwest.   Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches across the area, with some areas also receiving an inch of sleet.  The heaviest snowfall occurred along US Highway 50 from just south of Hutchinson to Newton to near Mount Hope, where almost 5 inches of snow fell.  Numerous auto accidents occurred from the mixture of freezing drizzle, sleet and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of sleet and snow fell across the county. One inch of sleet combined with 3 to 5 inches of snow to cause numerous auto accidents and slide offs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of April 2 a warm front was lifting into southern Kansas as mid level flow switched to the southwest. Warm moist air pushed northward up and over that front where steep mid level were in place. Elevated thunderstorms began to develop in this unstable environment. Large hail the size of quarters and golf balls were common with the storms that afternoon and evening. A few storms later that night were able to generate severe damaging winds down to the surface. This is very uncommon and these winds damaged several barns, trees, and power lines over a few counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system moved into the central United States, bringing several rounds of severe thunderstorms to the region.  Warm and moist air streamed northward out of the Gulf of Mexico on Saturday ahead of this system.  Strong thunderstorms quickly developed by mid-day and raced eastward across eastern Kansas and much of Missouri. Large hail up to the size of golf balls, damaging straight-line winds in excess of 60 mph, a brief tornado near Odessa, Missouri, and torrential rainfall moved through the area by mid-afternoon.||However, due to the speed and quick eastward progression of activity across the region Sunday afternoon, the atmosphere was able to recover to produce another round of severe thunderstorms across portions of eastern Kansas and western Missouri.  A second tornado occurred in the region near Ft. Scott, Kansas.  The formation and dissipation of the second tornado near Hume, MO was captured on live television by a Kansas City news station.  Minimal damage occurred as a result of the tornadoes, however isolated EF-2 damage occurred with the storm in east central Kansas. EF-0 damage was noted with the tornado near Odessa.

Drought conditions remain unchanged from March across northwest Kansas with the area continuing in D2 (Severe) to D3 (Extreme) Drought.  Precipitation during the month of April was one to one-and-a-half inches below normal in the Goodland, Sharon Springs, Tribune and Colby areas, while some locations further east fared better.  Hill City, Densmore and Norton received anywhere from three quarters of an inch to 1.70 inches above normal precipitation during April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cheyenne County continued in D3 (Extreme) Drought during April.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief landspout tornado was observed east of Colby, remaining over open fields and producing no known damage.

On the afternoon of April 2 a warm front was lifting into southern Kansas as mid level flow switched to the southwest. Warm moist air pushed northward up and over that front where steep mid level were in place. Elevated thunderstorms began to develop in this unstable environment. Large hail the size of quarters and golf balls were common with the storms that afternoon and evening. A few storms later that night were able to generate severe damaging winds down to the surface. This is very uncommon and these winds damaged several barns, trees, and power lines over a few counties.

An arctic air mass dropped south across the plains on March 1st, with a mixture of freezing drizzle and sleet.  This led to hazardous driving conditions across Kansas on March the 1st and March the 2nd, 2014.  The wintry mixture of freezing drizzle and sleet eventually transitioned to all snow as a significant upper level trough moved across the Midwest.   Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches across the area, with some areas also receiving an inch of sleet.  The heaviest snowfall occurred along US Highway 50 from just south of Hutchinson to Newton to near Mount Hope, where almost 5 inches of snow fell.  Numerous auto accidents occurred from the mixture of freezing drizzle, sleet and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of one to two inches of snow combined with a half an inch of sleet and some freezing drizzle to produce slick streets across the county.   Travel was significantly hampered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of light freezing rain pestered the Wichita Metro Area on January 31st. Although accumulations were minimal, accidents none-the-less occurred in South Wichita that morning. Fortunately, there were no injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain and freezing drizzle produced slick roads and highways, eventually leading to accidents in South Wichita. The accidents occurred on I-235, as well as on K-42 and South Seneca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system moved into the central United States, bringing several rounds of severe thunderstorms to the region.  Warm and moist air streamed northward out of the Gulf of Mexico on Saturday ahead of this system.  Strong thunderstorms quickly developed by mid-day and raced eastward across eastern Kansas and much of Missouri. Large hail up to the size of golf balls, damaging straight-line winds in excess of 60 mph, a brief tornado near Odessa, Missouri, and torrential rainfall moved through the area by mid-afternoon.||However, due to the speed and quick eastward progression of activity across the region Sunday afternoon, the atmosphere was able to recover to produce another round of severe thunderstorms across portions of eastern Kansas and western Missouri.  A second tornado occurred in the region near Ft. Scott, Kansas.  The formation and dissipation of the second tornado near Hume, MO was captured on live television by a Kansas City news station.  Minimal damage occurred as a result of the tornadoes, however isolated EF-2 damage occurred with the storm in east central Kansas. EF-0 damage was noted with the tornado near Odessa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually dry winter and spring, pushed portions of Central and South Central Kansas, back into extreme (D3) and severe drought (D2) for May 2104. The very dry conditions continued during the month of May, with some areas falling another half an inch to an inch below normal for rainfall, increasing the rainfall deficit for the year to over 4 inches below normal. Coupled with a dry March and April, the extreme drought (D3) increased across South Central Kansas.  Some welcome rainfall did begin to the return to portions of Central and South Central Kansas toward the end of the month that brought some relief from the drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another unusually dry month of May, with precipitation amounts almost 1 inch below normal, led to most of the county climbing into the extreme drought (D3) category. Some welcome rainfall fell late in the month, bringing some relief to drought stricken areas, but too late to help the winter wheat crop which was rated poor to well below average.

An unusually dry winter and spring, pushed portions of Central and South Central Kansas, back into extreme (D3) and severe drought (D2) for May 2104. The very dry conditions continued during the month of May, with some areas falling another half an inch to an inch below normal for rainfall, increasing the rainfall deficit for the year to over 4 inches below normal. Coupled with a dry March and April, the extreme drought (D3) increased across South Central Kansas.  Some welcome rainfall did begin to the return to portions of Central and South Central Kansas toward the end of the month that brought some relief from the drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another unusually dry month of May, with precipitation amounts almost 1 inch below normal, led to most of the county climbing into the extreme drought (D3) category. Some welcome rainfall fell late in the month, bringing some relief to drought stricken areas, but too late to help the winter wheat crop which was rated poor to well below average.

The supercell also produced a brief tornado touchdown near Severy, Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms and heavy rain moved across most of Southern Kansas during the early morning hours of June 7th, 2014.  Over 2 inches of rainfall fell in and around the Wichita metro area to produce flooding of city streets and low lying areas.   A complex of storms that occurred two days previous on June 5th, led to saturated soil conditions, which led to the rapid runoff of the water for the morning hours of June the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along 13th street from West street to Meridian.  Numerous  cars were submerged from the high water along 13th Street.  Rainfall amounts in the Wichita metro area measured 2.0 to 2.15 inches.  Most of the rainfall occurred between midnight and 4am.

In some cases these large trees damaged homes and vehicles.

Isolated supercell thunderstorms moved across portions of South Central Kansas on the evening of May 10th, 2014.  The thunderstorms developed in an unstable atmosphere ahead of a dry line and surface trough as it moved along the Kansas Turnpike.  The supercell thunderstorm that moved along Highway 54/400 was a prolific hail producer with some of the hail as large as golf balls.  The supercell also produced a brief tornado touchdown near Severy, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report was relayed via twitter on Teter Road.

Thunderstorms producing torrential rains moved east around 15 mph on the afternoon of June 24th. Flooding and flash flooding resulted. The most serious flash flooding occurred in South Wichita, more specifically 6 miles north-northwest of Haysville where the water reached the hoods of several cars. A couple of thunderstorms were marginally severe with 1 inch hail reported in extreme Northern Sedgwick County and in extreme Northwest Cowley County where 1 inch hail and a 60-mph gust were reported in Udall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred near East 2nd Street North and North St. Francis Street.

Two areas of thunderstorms moved across northwestern Kansas during the later evening hours and resulted in a couple of severe wind and hail reports. The first area of storms was initially a strong line of thunderstorms in eastern Colorado which weakened as it moved into Wallace County, producing a 60 mph wind gust near Wallace. Another area of storms developed rapidly just before 11:00 pm CDT from McCook Nebraska to near Hoxie Kansas and moved east into Norton and Graham Counties producing quarter size hail near Penokee.

Numerous thunderstorms drenched Southeast Kansas with 2 to around 5 inches of rain throughout the evening of the 28th that caused widespread flooding & flash flooding. The most serious flash flooding occurred in Central Montgomery County, where rescues from vehicles and homes were required in Independence, and in Elk County where U.S. Highway 160 was closed where flowing waters reached 3 feet deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down 3 miles south of Cherryvale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated supercell thunderstorms moved across portions of South Central Kansas on the evening of May 10th, 2014.  The thunderstorms developed in an unstable atmosphere ahead of a dry line and surface trough as it moved along the Kansas Turnpike.  The supercell thunderstorm that moved along Highway 54/400 was a prolific hail producer with some of the hail as large as golf balls.  The supercell also produced a brief tornado touchdown near Severy, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported the funnel cloud about halfway to the ground.

On the evening of July 7th a long line of thunderstorms moved southward through northern Kansas and Missouri, bringing widespread wind damage to the area. While the reports were numerous and widespread the magnitude of the damage was pretty minimal, mostly being relegated to minor structure damage and tree damage. These storms were predominantly wind producers, since there was very little in the way of large hail. The storms finally became sub-severe around midnight, bringing only widespread heavy rain through the overnight hours.

On the evening of July 7th a long line of thunderstorms moved southward through northern Kansas and Missouri, bringing widespread wind damage to the area. While the reports were numerous and widespread the magnitude of the damage was pretty minimal, mostly being relegated to minor structure damage and tree damage. The storms finally became sub-severe around midnight, bringing only widespread heavy rain through the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small-scale bow echo tracked southward out of Nebraska into North Central Kansas on this Wednesday evening. This bow echo, which evolved from a supercell, produced a long swath of significant severe weather over South Central Nebraska. This storm tracked across Jewell County between 8:30 p.m. and 9:45 p.m. CDT and was in the process of weakening. However, one final report of severe weather occurred in the town of Randall, where strong winds downed large tree limbs which resulted in some minor damage to one home.||This storm originally developed in an upslope flow regime, north of a quasi-stationary front, with high pressure over the Midwest. The front extended from northwest to southeast across Nebraska and bisected South Central Nebraska, separating temperatures in the 80s to near 90 from rain cooled air in the 60s and 70s during the late afternoon hours. Aloft, subtropical high pressure was over New Mexico, with a low amplitude ridge over the Rockies and a trough over the eastern United States. These storms formed on the eastern fringe of the instability gradient. Poor mid-level lapse rates, and cool surface temperatures, resulting in innocuous MLCAPE of 500 to 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was between 40 and 50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated near 60 MPH resulted in tree limbs being downed, breaking a window at one home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central Kansas during the evening hours of the Labor day holiday on September 1st, 2014.  The storms developed along an outflow boundary just to the southeast of Wichita, Kansas, in Sumner and Cowley counties.  The storms initially produced very large hail up to three inches in diameter.  But a supercell became tornadic, producing a brief tornado in Cowley County, Kansas south of the town of Dexter, and then produced another tornado with a longer track in Chautauqua county, Kansas.  This second tornado moved across open country, but did hit a couple of outbuildings and a barn at a farmstead, producing EF1 damage.  The supercell thunderstorms eventually began to bow out, and produced a swath of 90 mph damaging winds near Elgin, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged in size from three quarters of an inch to one inch.

Thunderstorms produced severe hail up to the size of golf balls on this Wednesday evening. Around 7:30 p.m. CDT, thunderstorms began forming across portions of North Central Kansas. The most severe storm developed over Rooks County by 8:00 p.m. CDT, with nickel and quarter size hail reported in and around Plainville shortly thereafter. The largest hail reported was the size of golf balls just west-southwest of town. Thunderstorms continued to form and increase in coverage, primarily south and east of Smith Center. However, the only other thunderstorm in which severe weather was reported occurred in eastern Smith and western Jewell counties, where quarter size hail was reported.||These storms formed right along a weak cold front that was merging with the lee side trough that had advanced out ahead of it. The upper level flow was fairly zonal over the central and western United States, with a shortwave trough progressing through the Northern Plains. Temperatures were in the lower to middle 80s when the storms developed, with dewpoints in the lower to middle 60s. Combined with steep 700-500 mb lapse rates (nearly 7.5 deg C/km), MLCAPE values were between 2000 and 3000 J/kg. Effective deep-layer shear was 25 to 30 knots.

During the mid to late evening hours on this Friday, a short bow echo tracked across North Central Kansas. The bow entered western Phillips and Rooks counties around 9:30 p.m. CDT, with the apex of the bow tracking through Phillips county. The line rapidly weakened as it moved east into Smith and Osborne counties, just after 10:30 p.m. CDT. Most of the severe winds occurred with the northern bookend vortex, just across the border in extreme southern Nebraska. However, there were two reports of 60 mph winds in western Phillips county, one of which was measured at Logan.||The bow echo formed over the High Plains of northeastern Colorado, north of the primary polar front that was stationary over the Southern Plains and south of a secondary front dropping through the Northern Plains. The flow aloft was fairly zonal with a weak embedded shortwave trough moving from Wyoming and Utah onto the High Plains by early evening. Late afternoon temperatures were in the lower to middle 80s with dewpoints in the lower 60s. This resulted in MLCAPE between 1000 and 1500 J/kg and 0-6 km shear was around 40 knots. By the time the convective line moved in, temperatures had dropped to around 80 degrees. Undoubtedly, further diurnal cooling limited the severity as the line progressed eastward.
Severe thunderstorms developed during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several city streets were completely under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of strong thunderstorms moved north and northeast during the afternoon and evening hours. Half dollar sized hail, wind damage and several reports of flash flooding were received. Strong winds broke windows in a residence and blew two semis off of Interstate 70.

Several clusters of strong thunderstorms moved north and northeast during the afternoon and evening hours. Half dollar sized hail, wind damage and several reports of flash flooding were received. Strong winds broke windows in a residence and blew two semis off of Interstate 70.

Storms developed rapidly during the afternoon over Cheyenne County Kansas and quickly evolved into a supercell thunderstorm which subsequently moved SSE.  Several brief tornadoes were confirmed with this storm, but they remained over open fields with no damage reported.  Other storms which developed over Kit Carson County in Colorado moved into Wallace County Kansas and produced strong winds to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated peak winds of 60 mph north of Weskan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central Kansas during the evening hours of the Labor day holiday on September 1st, 2014.  The storms developed along an outflow boundary just to the southeast of Wichita, Kansas, in Sumner and Cowley counties.  The storms initially produced very large hail up to three inches in diameter.  But a supercell became tornadic, producing a brief tornado in Cowley County, Kansas south of the town of Dexter, and then produced another tornado with a longer track in Chautauqua county, Kansas.  This second tornado moved across open country, but did hit a couple of outbuildings and a barn at a farmstead, producing EF1 damage.  The supercell thunderstorms eventually began to bow out, and produced a swath of 90 mph damaging winds near Elgin, Kansas.

Supercell thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central Kansas during the evening hours of the Labor day holiday on September 1st, 2014.  The storms developed along an outflow boundary just to the southeast of Wichita, Kansas, in Sumner and Cowley counties.  The storms initially produced very large hail up to three inches in diameter.  But a supercell became tornadic, producing a brief tornado in Cowley County, Kansas south of the town of Dexter, and then produced another tornado with a longer track in Chautauqua county, Kansas.  This second tornado moved across open country, but did hit a couple of outbuildings and a barn at a farmstead, producing EF1 damage.  The supercell thunderstorms eventually began to bow out, and produced a swath of 90 mph damaging winds near Elgin, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail ranged in size from nickel to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a three-week minimum in severe thunderstorm activity within this six-county North Central Kansas area, the month of June kicked off with a relatively widespread severe episode centered during the late afternoon hours on this Sunday. In general, this event consisted of several small clusters of multicell storms gradually morphing into a more organized, east-southeast surging squall line. Ground-truth storm reports consisted of a fairly even mix of hail and winds. Hail-wise, stones up to golf ball size were reported in Alton and up to ping pong ball size in Osborne. All known severe-criteria wind reports emanated from Osborne and Jewell counties, including estimated gusts of 70 MPH in Formoso which caused tree damage. On a positive note, this event brought a much-needed soaking rain of generally 1-3 to the majority of the local area. Localized spots slightly exceeded 3, including CoCoRaHS and NWS cooperative observer 24-hour totals featuring 3.40 two miles southwest of Alton and 3.23 at both Ionia and seven miles northwest of Lebanon. ||Rather weak, non-severe elevated thunderstorms got underway across parts of the area fairly early in the day between 10 am-1 pm CDT. During the 1-4 pm CDT time frame, transient clusters of storms gradually intensified and became strong to marginally severe as they tracked northeastward over the area. The ensuing three hours between 4-7 pm CDT then featured the main event of the day as ongoing storm clusters over North Central Kansas gradually grew upscale into a linear complex that also merged with a small line segment dropping southeast out of southern Nebraska. By the time this maturing squall line reached the Jewell, Mitchell and southeastern Osborne counties area, the large hail threat declined as strong winds took center stage. By 830 pm CDT, nearly all convection had vacated the local area into central and northeastern Kansas. In the big picture aloft, this event was driven by a series of low amplitude shortwave disturbances ejecting eastward out of the Central Rockies into the Central Plains, along the southeastern periphery of a parent trough shifting eastward from the Northern Rockies. At the surface, convection intensified ahead of and along the combination of a convectively-driven outflow boundary and synoptic-scale cold front gradually working southeastward across the region. Although early-day convection and resultant cloud cover kept much of North Central Kansas from realizing its full potential for low level destabilization, parts of the area were able to at least briefly realize high temperatures in the 80s F, along with dewpoints in the low-mid 60s. As storms intensified during the afternoon hours, the mesoscale environment featured steep mid level lapse rates, mixed layer CAPE up to around 2000 J/kg and increasing 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of at least 30-40 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a three-week minimum in severe thunderstorm activity within this six-county North Central Kansas area, the month of June kicked off with a relatively widespread severe episode centered during the late afternoon hours on this Sunday. In general, this event consisted of several small clusters of multicell storms gradually morphing into a more organized, east-southeast surging squall line. Ground-truth storm reports consisted of a fairly even mix of hail and winds. Hail-wise, stones up to golf ball size were reported in Alton and up to ping pong ball size in Osborne. All known severe-criteria wind reports emanated from Osborne and Jewell counties, including estimated gusts of 70 MPH in Formoso which caused tree damage. On a positive note, this event brought a much-needed soaking rain of generally 1-3 to the majority of the local area. Localized spots slightly exceeded 3, including CoCoRaHS and NWS cooperative observer 24-hour totals featuring 3.40 two miles southwest of Alton and 3.23 at both Ionia and seven miles northwest of Lebanon. ||Rather weak, non-severe elevated thunderstorms got underway across parts of the area fairly early in the day between 10 am-1 pm CDT. During the 1-4 pm CDT time frame, transient clusters of storms gradually intensified and became strong to marginally severe as they tracked northeastward over the area. The ensuing three hours between 4-7 pm CDT then featured the main event of the day as ongoing storm clusters over North Central Kansas gradually grew upscale into a linear complex that also merged with a small line segment dropping southeast out of southern Nebraska. By the time this maturing squall line reached the Jewell, Mitchell and southeastern Osborne counties area, the large hail threat declined as strong winds took center stage. By 830 pm CDT, nearly all convection had vacated the local area into central and northeastern Kansas. In the big picture aloft, this event was driven by a series of low amplitude shortwave disturbances ejecting eastward out of the Central Rockies into the Central Plains, along the southeastern periphery of a parent trough shifting eastward from the Northern Rockies. At the surface, convection intensified ahead of and along the combination of a convectively-driven outflow boundary and synoptic-scale cold front gradually working southeastward across the region. Although early-day convection and resultant cloud cover kept much of North Central Kansas from realizing its full potential for low level destabilization, parts of the area were able to at least briefly realize high temperatures in the 80s F, along with dewpoints in the low-mid 60s. As storms intensified during the afternoon hours, the mesoscale environment featured steep mid level lapse rates, mixed layer CAPE up to around 2000 J/kg and increasing 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear of at least 30-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total rainfall amount of 3.40 inches was reported.
