This was a fairly ripe setup for slow-moving, heavy-rain producing storms. In the mid and upper levels, small scale disturbances lifted across the local area, slightly downstream from a parent trough/closed low approaching from Colorado/Wyoming. Meanwhile, the local airmass was quite moist, characterized by impressive precipitable water values generally in the 1.7 to 2 inch range. The increasing coverage of convection toward nightfall was aided by enhanced moisture transport along the axis of a southerly low level jet accelerating to 30-40 knots. Although most unstable CAPE values averaged around 1000 J/kg, weak mid level lapse rates and deep-layer wind shear promoted more of a heavy rain versus severe weather threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately the northwestern one-third of Rooks County received 2-5 inches of rain, with locally higher totals. A total of 3.5 inches of rain was reported just within a three-hour period (between 753 pm CDT and 1053 CDT), about 6 miles north of Stockton. Also, an event-total of 5.78 fell 10 miles west-northwest of Stockton per a CoCoRaHS observer. Also, minor street flooding was reported in Stockton during this time period, as well as other nearby county roads. The bulk of rain occurred between 9 pm CDT and midnight CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primarily between 7 pm-1 am CDT on this Saturday evening, much of this six-county North Central Kansas area observed widespread thunderstorm activity. However, all severe-criteria hail and wind reports, along with the heaviest pockets of torrential rainfall, targeted Jewell County along with northern and eastern portions of Smith and Mitchell counties. All severe reports in these areas occurred between 8-10 pm CDT in association with a southeastward-drifting supercell cluster that first blossomed over Franklin County Nebraska before crossing the state line into the local area. As this initially rather discrete severe storm affected Smith County mainly between 8-9 pm CDT, it resulted in several reports of quarter to golf ball size hail in the Bellaire and Lebanon areas, and even one baseball size report just south of Lebanon. In that same portion of Smith County, estimated wind gusts up to around 80 MPH downed several power poles. Even slightly outside the main path of this storm, severe outflow winds of 59 MPH were measured at Smith Center airport. As the initial supercell merged into newly-developing convection over Jewell and northern Mitchell counties to become a larger-scale cluster, additional severe reports included estimated 60 MPH winds at Burr Oak and half dollar size hail several miles east of Beloit. At the same time severe weather was ongoing in Jewell and Mitchell counties, additional strong storms flared up farther west across parts of Phillips, Rooks, Smith and Osborne counties between 9-10 pm CDT. After 11 pm CDT storms across the local area weakened considerably, and by 2 am CDT the entire area was essentially rain-free. ||Rainfall-wise, the primary severe storm cluster produced three distinct, albeit small bullseyes of very heavy rainfall of generally 3-5. One of these was in northeastern Smith County, another in south central Jewell County where the NWS cooperative observer at Ionia measured 4.36, and the third one in northeastern Mitchell County where a CoCoRaHS observer reported 5.00 approximately five miles east of Beloit. Despite these pockets of very heavy rain there were very few ground-truth reports of flooding, although the Solomon River near Simpson crested only a few inches below flood stage on Sunday the 10th. ||This was a fairly typical late-summer severe weather event featuring fairly high instability values but only modest deep layer wind shear and weak large scale forcing. In the mid to upper levels, generally quasi-zonal flow was in place over the Central Plains, containing one or more fairly subtle shortwave troughs. At the surface, storms initiated in the vicinity of a northwest-southeast oriented quasi stationary front draped through the local area along an axis from western Nebraska to southeast Kansas, with this front partially comprised of a remnant outflow boundary from storms the night before. With surface dewpoints well into the 60s F across the local area, most-unstable CAPE climbed as high as 2000-3000 J/kg. Although at least one storm in the local area briefly exhibited supercell structure, 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear only averaging around 30 knots promoted a predominant multicell/quasi-linear mode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 24-hour rainfall amount was 3.24 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a 3.5 hour period centered from 7-1030 pm CDT on this Sunday evening, a pair of strong to severe linear mesoscale convective systems (MCS) affected this six-county North Central Kansas area and merged with each other in the process. These storms were primarily damaging wind-producers. However, the one known severe-criteria hail report was a significant one as golf ball to softball size stones occurred near Agra. Wind-wise, the initial storm complex that moved into the area from northwest Kansas yielded severe-criteria measured gusts at Logan (58 MPH) and Phillipsburg airport (67 MPH). A bit later in the evening, following the merger of the first storm complex with a stronger, larger MCS diving southeastward out of south central Nebraska, Jewell and Mitchell counties bore the brunt of damaging winds including estimated 70 MPH winds in Mankato, a measured 86 MPH gust near Jewell, and a report of tree branches downed throughout Beloit. Rainfall-wise, the progressive nature of convection held amounts under 1 inch in most places.  ||Timing-wise, the first storm complex tracked into Phillips and Rooks counties from the west between 7-8 pm CDT, having grown upscale from its humble late-afternoon beginnings along western portions of the Kansas-Nebraska border. As this initial area of convection reached far eastern portions of Smith and Osborne counties around 930 pm CDT, its northern end quickly merged with the leading edges of a stronger linear MCS charging southeastward out of Nebraska. During the ensuing hour, strong to severe winds swept through Jewell and Mitchell counties before departing the local area to the east and southeast. Although showers/weak storms lingered over some places a bit longer, the vast majority of the local area was void of precipitation by 1230 am CDT on the 18th. ||By late-summer standards, this synoptic and mesoscale setup was fairly ripe for an organized severe weather event. In the mid-upper levels, a low-amplitude shortwave trough brushed into the Central Plains from the northwest, along the southern periphery of a larger scale disturbance centered over southern Saskatchewan/Manitoba. As a result, mid-upper level flow increased enough to promote generally 30-35 knots of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear over North Central Kansas. In addition, instability was quite potent as the combination of afternoon surface temperatures in the 90s F and dewpoints in the 60s promoted mixed-layer CAPE values of 2000-4000 J/kg ahead of the incoming convective complexes.

For a few hours on this Sunday afternoon, a concentrated zone of several strong to severe thunderstorms streamed through eastern portions North Central Kansas, almost exclusively affecting Mitchell and eastern Jewell counties within the local area. Based on a number of storm reports, this was mainly a hail and heavy rain/flooding event, as there were only a few reports of slightly sub-severe wind gusts. Hail-wise, there were several reports of stones ranging from quarter all the way up to tennis ball size, primarily within a 10-mile radius of Beloit, and with stones to at least ping pong ball size falling in the city itself. Overall though, the biggest story of the afternoon ended up being flooding, as the slow-moving, training corridor of intense convection dumped at least 1.50-2.50 of rain over much of Mitchell and far southeastern Jewell counties, with a concentrated zone of higher totals right around 3 centered within and very near Beloit. Given that most of this rain fell within a two-hour period between 4-6 pm CDT, flash flooding ensued in the immediate Beloit area, including water covering portions of Highways 24 and 14. ||In the days and even hours leading up to this event, it appeared that severe thunderstorms might develop farther west and thus pose an overall more widespread and significant threat within the local six-county area. However, a slow-moving, northeast-southwest oriented cold front shifted through most of the area during the day, and by the time convective initiation occurred, this primary low-level convergence zone focused severe storms only within the far-eastern local counties. Timing-wise, severe convection first flared up between 3-4 pm CDT, as seasonably-strong forcing along the front quickly resulted in a messy storm mode with numerous cell mergers and eventually an almost solid line of convection. On a positive note, this storm mode helped prevent the establishment of discrete supercells and a potential tornadic threat. Locally, storm coverage peaked between 400-630 pm CDT, before the main corridor of convection gradually departed southward and eastward into central and northeast Kansas. Although additional thunderstorms affected parts of North Central Kansas well into the night, none of them were severe. ||On the synoptic and mesoscale levels, this was a rather potent late-summer severe weather setup. In the mid and upper levels, a broad, seasonably strong trough shifted eastward out of the Rockies into northern Kansas, accompanied by an upper level jet streak and resultant increase in upper level divergence. Meanwhile, low-level forcing focused along the aforementioned cold front as surface temperatures climbed to around 90 F and dewpoints averaged well into the 60s. Instability and shear parameters were certainly supportive of supercell development along the front, as mixed-layer CAPE climbed into the 2000-3000 J/kg range in the presence of 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear around 40 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust upper level wave matured over the Rockies and moved eastward across the Central Plains during the afternoon and evening hours of October 1st, 2014. Unseasonably rich gulf moisture streamed north ahead of this system, combined with strong daytime heating to produce severe storms and supercells across portions of Central and South Central Kansas. The intersection of the warm front and dry line produced enough shear to produce some low topped supercells with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was driven by 55 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell produced a few reports of large hail.

Several rounds of thunderstorms, producing large hail, damaging winds and heavy rains, developed over eastern Kansas and tracked east into western Missouri during the afternoon and evening hours of October 1st. The storms developed in the vicinity of a quasi-stationary boundary as an upper-level shortwave trough tracked through the region.

Scattered thunderstorms develop across north central Kansas late on this Tuesday afternoon, with one turning severe weather over Jewell County. This storm formed over northeastern Smith County around 5:30 p.m. CDT and intensified as it progressed into northern Jewell County by 6 p.m. CDT. Hail up to the size of quarters occurred over the northwest part of the county, near the Nebraska-Kansas state line. Similar size hail and tree branches were blown down a little east of Lovewell Reservoir. Most of the storm was east of the county by 7:30 p.m. CDT.||The flow aloft was zonal but exhibited a broad trough across the northern United States. An embedded shortwave trough was advancing through the Pacific Northwest. A suppressed subtropical high was over Texas. A strong surface cold front extended from eastern Canada across the Northern Plains and into the Northern Rockies. This front very slowly sagged southward into north central Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours. Immediately prior to thunderstorm development, temperatures were in the 90s, with dewpoints in the 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor, only resulting in MLCAPE nearing 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 35-40 knots.

Other small thunderstorms also developed as the initial storm propagated southeastward into Phillips, Rooks, and Osborne counties. The coverage was very limited. As the small cluster of storms moved into northwestern Osborne County, it briefly produced hail in and near the town of Alton. The largest hail reported was the size of golf balls. This cluster continued moving eastward through Osborne and Mitchell counties, but no additional severe weather was reported.||Northwest flow was over the Central Plains with a ridge over the West and the trough over the East. This storm cluster developed north of a quasi-stationary front that extended from the Front Range of the Rockies and then southeastward across western and southern Kansas. They appeared to form along the front at 850 mb, where a tight moisture gradient existed and southwest winds were maintaining the flow of rich dewpoints up the frontal surface. MUCAPE values were around 1000 J/kg with mid-level lapse rates of 7 degrees C/km. Deep layer shear was approximately 35 knots.

A vigorous cold front dropped south across Central Kansas during the early morning hours of September 1st, 2014.  This cold front led to a convective complex of storms moving along this boundary to initially produce large hail and damaging winds.  Additional thunderstorms developed along the stalled out boundary during the predawn hours to produce heavy rainfall across Central Kansas.  Some locations reported up to 6 inches of rainfall by the late morning hours.   This severe weather episode is a continuation of an event that started during the evening hours of August 31st, 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs of unknown size were blown down on Highway 177 near Matfield Green. The limbs were large enough that a chainsaw was needed to cut them up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortly before sunrise on this Thursday morning this six-county North Central Kansas area endured its final severe thunderstorm of 2014, and considering the time of day, the semi-discrete storm's surprising furiosity made for a noteworthy event. In a mesoscale environment that appeared mainly supportive of non-severe elevated convection, this intense storm gradually became more surface-based as it first crossed southern Osborne County between 515-610 am CDT and then tore across the heart of Mitchell County from 610-700 am CDT before exiting the local area. While no verifying ground-truth severe weather reports were received from within Osborne and western Mitchell counties, that changed over eastern Mitchell County where there were numerous reports of large hail up to golf ball size, a measured wind gust of 73 MPH and estimated gusts up to 90 MPH. Not surprisingly, significant crop damage and some structure damage was reported, including windows broken out of homes and a destroyed farm Quonset building near Asherville. Also near Asherville, a semi truck slid off of Highway 24 in an area where golf ball size hail covered the road. The most intense core of this storm passed only a few miles to the south and east of Beloit, narrowly sparing the city. ||Before this storm made early-morning headlines, it initially appeared that this entire North Central Kansas area would miss out on severe weather given that other clusters of severe convection during the preceding 12 hours instead focused just to the north over south central Nebraska and to the east within northeast Kansas. However, as increasing large-scale ascent pushed into Kansas from the west during the pre-dawn hours and intercepted the western fringes of a modest instability axis, a small line of storms first showed signs of intensification in the Ellis and southern Rooks County area around 5 am CDT. Within a short time, a portion of this line morphed into a semi-discrete strong to severe storm across southern Osborne County before really taking off over Mitchell County after 6 am CDT. On the synoptic scale aloft, this early-autumn event focused just downstream from a sharpening mid-upper trough axis aligned at sunrise from the central Dakotas to a mid level vort max over southeast Colorado. At the surface, this storm was post-frontal, as it formed slightly behind a modest cold front that passed through North Central Kansas during the night. Instability-wise, there was seemingly little support for such a robust early-morning storm given that most-unstable CAPE was only analyzed to be around 500 J/kg in the presence of surface dewpoints generally in the mid-50s F. However, 0-6 kilometer deep layer wind shear was certainly supportive of potential severe weather, having risen into the 50-60 knot range ahead of the approaching trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 635 am CDT, law enforcement reported that windows were broken from wind-driven hail on the south side of homes about 3 to 8 miles south of Beloit. These homes were located approximately from near Road L to Road P, just east of Highway 14. At 640 am CDT, a trained spotter reported golf ball size hail along with estimated 60 mph wind gusts, 5 miles south of Beloit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system and a dry line produced several rounds of severe thunderstorms with some super cells. There were reports of a couple tornadoes, wind damage, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey conducted by the NWS Office in Pleasant Hill found an EF1 tornado began at the intersection of Highway 69 and Soldier Road, near Hammond, Kansas. Minor damage was noted near Hammond, with the most significant damage consisting of toppled over grain bins and several derailed empty train cars. Through the remainder of the Bourbon County path, several destroyed outbuildings were noted as well as significant tree damage. The tornado path continued into Linn County, Kansas. Maximum winds with the tornado in Bourbon County were estimated to be between 100 and 110 mph with a maximum width of 200 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe winds and hail were reported in extreme southern Mitchell County late on this Tuesday afternoon. A fairly narrow north-south oriented band of rain progressed through Nebraska and Kansas during the day, eventually departing into Iowa. In its wake, during the late afternoon hours, scattered thunderstorms developed from central Kansas northeastward into southeastern Nebraska. One of these storms formed over Russell County and moved northeast across Lincoln County. The storm then took on supercell characteristics and began propagating eastward along the Mitchell-Lincoln county border. This storm had a history of producing severe weather over Lincoln County, with hail up to the size of tennis balls. Hail as large as quarters occurred over extreme southeastern Mitchell county, along with estimated winds of 60 MPH.||The flow aloft was characterized by a trough over the West, with the leading nose of a 75 knot jet streak along the Colorado-New Mexico border, and a ridge was over the east. A shortwave trough was on the poleward side of the jet streak with a closed 500 mb low over North Dakota. North central Kansas was in the warm sector of low pressure that lifted north-northeast through the Dakotas during the day. The associated cold front remained quasi-stationary across western Kansas. Just prior to thunderstorm formation, temperatures were in the 70s with dewpoints in the 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor and when combined with the surface environment, resulted in MLCAPE of only 1000 J/kg. However, low-level winds were backed and from the southeast. This resulted in deep layer shear between 40 and 45 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 60 MPH and were accompanied by quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone thunderstorm produced low-end severe hail on this Wednesday evening. A couple very isolated thunderstorms developed over North Central Kansas shortly after 6 p.m. CDT. The storm that eventually produced the hail formed on the Sheridan-Graham county border. It subsequently advanced eastward, moving into Rooks County around 7:30 p.m. CDT. One inch hail occurred just northwest of Stockton about 20 minutes later. The storm then weakened and eventually dissipated over Obsorne County in the following hour.||The flow aloft was typical of summertime, with the polar jet zonal in character along the U.S.-Canada border and subtropical high pressure over the southern half of the Country. A relatively potent shortwave trough was progressing through the Pacific Northwest. A low pressure system was associated with this trough over Montana. Nebraska was in the warm sector, between the warm front and a leeside trough/dryline that extended southward through western Nebraska. It had been a hot day, with high temperatures breaking 100 degrees. As the storms moved in, temperatures were in the lower 90s with dewpoints in the lower 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were steep (7.5 degrees C/km). Combined with the boundary layer thermodynamic environment, this resulted in MLCAPE around 2500 J/kg. Despite the region being located south of the polar jet, deep layer shear was around 35 knots.

Scattered thunderstorms develop across north central Kansas late on this Tuesday afternoon, with one turning severe weather over Jewell County. This storm formed over northeastern Smith County around 5:30 p.m. CDT and intensified as it progressed into northern Jewell County by 6 p.m. CDT. Hail up to the size of quarters occurred over the northwest part of the county, near the Nebraska-Kansas state line. Similar size hail and tree branches were blown down a little east of Lovewell Reservoir. Most of the storm was east of the county by 7:30 p.m. CDT.||The flow aloft was zonal but exhibited a broad trough across the northern United States. An embedded shortwave trough was advancing through the Pacific Northwest. A suppressed subtropical high was over Texas. A strong surface cold front extended from eastern Canada across the Northern Plains and into the Northern Rockies. This front very slowly sagged southward into north central Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours. Immediately prior to thunderstorm development, temperatures were in the 90s, with dewpoints in the 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor, only resulting in MLCAPE nearing 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 35-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be near 65 MPH and were accompanied by quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms develop across north central Kansas late on this Tuesday afternoon, with one turning severe weather over Jewell County. This storm formed over northeastern Smith County around 5:30 p.m. CDT and intensified as it progressed into northern Jewell County by 6 p.m. CDT. Hail up to the size of quarters occurred over the northwest part of the county, near the Nebraska-Kansas state line. Similar size hail and tree branches were blown down a little east of Lovewell Reservoir. Most of the storm was east of the county by 7:30 p.m. CDT.||The flow aloft was zonal but exhibited a broad trough across the northern United States. An embedded shortwave trough was advancing through the Pacific Northwest. A suppressed subtropical high was over Texas. A strong surface cold front extended from eastern Canada across the Northern Plains and into the Northern Rockies. This front very slowly sagged southward into north central Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours. Immediately prior to thunderstorm development, temperatures were in the 90s, with dewpoints in the 60s. Mid-level lapse rates were poor, only resulting in MLCAPE nearing 1500 J/kg. Deep layer shear was 35-40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was accompanied by winds near 65 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small storm cluster produced severe hail very late on this Wednesday evening. A few isolated thunderstorms had been ongoing through the evening hours over northwestern Kansas. These storms had almost entirely dissipated by 9 p.m. CDT. However, a new thunderstorm formed on the Graham-Norton county border shortly thereafter. Other small thunderstorms also developed as the initial storm propagated southeastward into Phillips, Rooks, and Osborne counties. The coverage was very limited. As the small cluster of storms moved into northwestern Osborne County, it briefly produced hail in and near the town of Alton. The largest hail reported was the size of golf balls. This cluster continued moving eastward through Osborne and Mitchell counties, but no additional severe weather was reported.||Northwest flow was over the Central Plains with a ridge over the West and the trough over the East. This storm cluster developed north of a quasi-stationary front that extended from the Front Range of the Rockies and then southeastward across western and southern Kansas. They appeared to form along the front at 850 mb, where a tight moisture gradient existed and southwest winds were maintaining the flow of rich dewpoints up the frontal surface. MUCAPE values were around 1000 J/kg with mid-level lapse rates of 7 degrees C/km. Deep layer shear was approximately 35 knots.

A vigorous cold front dropped south across Central Kansas during the early morning hours of September 1st, 2014.  This cold front led to a convective complex of storms moving along this boundary to initially produce large hail and damaging winds.  Additional thunderstorms developed along the stalled out boundary during the predawn hours to produce heavy rainfall across Central Kansas.  Some locations reported up to 6 inches of rainfall by the late morning hours.   This severe weather episode is a continuation of an event that started during the evening hours of August 31st, 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer reported 3.77 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rains caused widespread flooding across the southern sections of Montgomery and Labette counties.  Two water rescues were executed in Montgomery county near Coffeyville and a family was evacuated near Pumpkin creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was street flooding and road washouts between Chautauqua and Elgin.  The Little Caney river was out of the banks near Niotaze and flooding the side roads.  Flooding was also noted on both sides of Highway 166.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rains caused widespread flooding across the southern sections of Montgomery and Labette counties.  Two water rescues were executed in Montgomery county near Coffeyville and a family was evacuated near Pumpkin creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two water rescues occurred in Coffeyville area and a family was evacuated from their home along the Pumpkin creek.  There was a submerged vehicle along the Onion creek south of Coffeyville as well. No injuries were reported. County road 3900 near Dearing was also closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust upper level wave matured over the Rockies and moved eastward across the Central Plains during the afternoon and evening hours of October 1st, 2014. Unseasonably rich gulf moisture streamed north ahead of this system, combined with strong daytime heating to produce severe storms and supercells across portions of Central and South Central Kansas. The intersection of the warm front and dry line produced enough shear to produce some low topped supercells with large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported near Bartlett as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact winter weather system moved across portions of the plains during the late evening hours of February 9th and the early morning hours of February 10th, 2014. As this system moved across, portions of Central and South Central  Kansas received 2 to 5 inches of snow. The heaviest snowfall occurred along a swath from Great Bend, Kansas to Wichita to Chanute, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow fell across the northern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward advancing warm front moved into portions of Southern Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours of April 2nd, 2014.   As this boundary moved north, showers and thunderstorms developed along it or to the south of it, generally south of US highway 54/400.  The storms developed into supercell thunderstorms, which produced large hail as large as two inches hit much of Allen County, especially over the city of Iola.   In South Central Kansas 2 inch hail impacted the Kansas Star Casino near Mulvane.  A brief tornado touchdown also occurred 5 miles southwest of Elk City in Chautauqua County, Kansas.||Multiple storms trained over Allen county, producing 3.77 inches of rain at the Iola Water Plant.  This abundance of rain caused county flooding and even some river flooding along the Neosho River.

A strong upper level storm system approached the plains from the southwest during the day on February 3rd, 2014.  By late evening, snow began to fall over extreme South Central Kansas.  After midnight on February 4th, 2014, snow spread across South Central and Central Kansas, especially along a line from Hutchinson to Salina.   By daybreak on February 4th, 2014,  snow rates increased across South Central Kansas.  Snow persisted throughout the day and into the evening hours resulting in snowfall totals up to nearly a foot in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow fell across the county.   The heaviest snow amounts were across the northern portion of the county.   Numerous auto accidents and slide offs were reported.   Blowing and drifting of the snow made county roads almost impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm moved into southwest and south central Kansas the night of Monday, February 3rd, and increased in intensity during the day Tuesday, February 4th. There was snow, moderate to heavy at times, especially across south central Kansas Tuesday afternoon.  Before the storm ended late Tuesday night the 4th, there was a small area of 11-inch snowfall covering southern Pratt and northern Barber Counties.  There was a pocket of 8 to 10 inches of snow from northeast Edwards County to northwest Kiowa County, as well as a 10-inch band of snow from northern Meade into eastern Seward Counties.  Yet another small area of 8 inch snowfall fell in southwestern Barber County.  Outside of the heavier snowfall, from 4 to 8 inches of snow fell southeast of line from just west of Liberal to just north of Garden City to west of Hays.  Lesser amounts of 2 to 3 inches of snow fell just northwest of that line, from Hugoton, to near Lakin to Ness City to near Wakeeney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8.3 inches was reported at a location 7 miles northwest of Coldwater.

During the early to mid-morning on Tuesday the 4th, accumulating snow overspread North Central Kansas from the south, peaking in intensity between the late morning and early evening hours before gradually diminishing overnight and ending before sunrise on the 5th. While nearly the entire six-county local area received at least 3-5 inches of snow, Rooks, Osborne, Jewell and Mitchell counties reported more widespread amounts of 4-6, and localized higher totals including 8 measured by the NWS cooperative observer at Covert rural station in Osborne County. A few of the other highest cooperative observer snow totals within the area featured 7.2 four miles west-northwest of Plainville, 6.5 at Hunter and 6.1 at Jewell. ||Thanks to cold temperatures that only averaged between 15-25 degrees during most of the event, snow was fairly light and fluffy in character. Winds were also an issue, as sustained northerly speeds of generally 15-25 MPH and gusts to around 35 MPH promoted drifting snow and frequent visibility reductions to around one-half mile within the heaviest snow bands. The combination of winds and falling temperatures dropped wind chill values into the zero to -15 range from the late afternoon into the overnight hours. Snow first started pushing northward out of central Kansas into the local area mainly between 5-8 AM, but the majority of impactful, accumulating snow fell during the 9 AM-6 PM time frame, with only minor accumulations lingering into the night. On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was driven by a large, amplified trough that tracked northeast through the Central and Southern Plains. Given that the closed 700 millibar low pressure center tracked across the southern half of Kansas into Missouri, the local area was spared the highest snow totals of at least 9-12 that targeted parts of central and northeast Kansas. At the surface, a pronounced Arctic cold front plunged southward through North Central Kansas during the day, ushering in the frigid temperatures and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 6.5 inches fell in the community of Hunter.  Also, a snowfall total of 6.0 inches fell in the community of Beloit. A lesser amount of 4.5 inches was reported in Cawker City and Glen Elder Lake.

According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, March 2014 marked the 22nd-consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued some portion of this six-county North Central Kansas area, continuing one of the most notable droughts in decades. For parts of the area, March was likely at least the fifth-consecutive month of below normal precipitation, but compared to 30-year normals it was hands-down the driest month of all recent ones. According to reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers, and also taking into account radar-derived precipitation estimates, this entire North Central Kansas area measured below normal March precipitation (based on long-term March normals of roughly 1.85). However, more significantly, the vast majority of the area received less than one-fifth of normal March precipitation. Given these dubious March precipitation trends, the weekly U.S. Drought Monitor kept D2 Severe Drought firmly intact within all of Phillips, Rooks and Osborne counties, most of Smith County and mainly western portions of Jewell and Mitchell counties. Meanwhile, the majority of Jewell and Mitchell counties remained in D1 Moderate Drought. ||Not surprisingly, much of the area endured one of the Top-15 driest months of March on record, including Smith Center with its 11th-driest out of 104 years with only 0.12, and Beloit (0.20) with its 14th-driest out of 106 years of complete records. As for monthly extremes, a few of the lowest March totals per NWS cooperative observers included only 0.02 at Hunter, 0.06 at Webster Dam and 0.12 at Smith Center and Lebanon.  Although still extremely dry versus normal, the ���wettest��� March stations were located one mile north of Long Island (0.32), four miles west-northwest of Plainville (0.26) and in Mankato (0.25). According to the USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report for the week ending March 30th, the recent dry, windy conditions had caused a drop in pond water levels.  Specifically in the north central district, topsoil moisture supplies rated 73% short to very short and only 26% adequate, while subsoil moisture was even worse off at 77% short to very short and just 23% adequate.

A northward advancing warm front moved into portions of Southern Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours of April 2nd, 2014.   As this boundary moved north, showers and thunderstorms developed along it or to the south of it, generally south of US highway 54/400.  The storms developed into supercell thunderstorms, which produced large hail as large as two inches hit much of Allen County, especially over the city of Iola.   In South Central Kansas 2 inch hail impacted the Kansas Star Casino near Mulvane.  A brief tornado touchdown also occurred 5 miles southwest of Elk City in Chautauqua County, Kansas.||Multiple storms trained over Allen county, producing 3.77 inches of rain at the Iola Water Plant.  This abundance of rain caused county flooding and even some river flooding along the Neosho River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous low water crossings were reported with water over them.  Street flooding was also reported in the town of Iola, Kansas.

Drought conditions continued across all of northwest Kansas during January. The area is rated as being in D2 (Severe) to D3 (Extreme) drought. Precipitation during the month was generally below normal although some higher amounts in Cheyenne county were above normal where observers reported three quarters of an inch of moisture.  While amounts ranged from 0.10 to 0.80 inches, most totals were only 0.20 to 0.30 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gove county is rated as being in D2 (Severe) drought.

Starting during the late evening hours of Saturday the 22nd, peaking in intensity overnight and gradually moving out by late morning on the 23rd, a notably-narrow band of heavy snow in the 6-8 inch range targeted mainly the northern halves of Phillips and Smith counties, with isolated 6 amounts also reported as far east as central Jewell County.  Meanwhile, southern portions of these counties, along with all of Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties observed little to no accumulation. Within this six-county portion of North Central Kansas, the primary heavy snow band generally extended no more than 5-10 miles either side of a line stretched from near Long Island to Mankato. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest official snow totals featured 8.9 at Lebanon, 8.0 at Smith Center and 6.5 at Mankato. Only a short distance south of the primary band, locations such as Logan and Beloit hardly saw a single snowflake. Fortunately, winds were not a major issue during this event, with sustained northeasterly speeds of generally 10-15 MPH keeping blowing and drifting to a minimum. ||Timing-wise, this quasi-stationary heavy snow band first started building southward into extreme North Central Kansas out of southern Nebraska during the 10 PM-12 AM time frame, peaked in intensity during the pre-dawn hours, and gradually weakened and slid off to the east during the morning of the 23rd. Not surprisingly, the meteorological culprit for this narrow, banded snow event was a concentrated zone of mid level frontogenesis evident at the 700 millibar level, driven by lift from passing upper level jet streaks. On the larger scale aloft, a broad, low-amplitude trough encompassed most of the nation. At the surface during this snow event, North Central Kansas became increasingly under the influence of high pressure building southward out of southern Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 6.0 inches occurred 1 mile north of Long Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system and a dry line produced several rounds of severe thunderstorms with some super cells. There were reports of a couple tornadoes, wind damage, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey team determined an EF2 tornado struck the town of Baxter Springs, Kansas. The tornado started near Quapaw, Oklahoma and crossed the state line into Kansas then moved through the town of Baxter Springs producing a 150 yard wide swath of damage from southwest to northeast through the center of town.  Emergency management reported approximately 90 homes and 11 business were destroyed and an additional seven businesses and 85 homes suffered damage. Approximately 25 injuries were reported but no fatalities. Maximum wind speeds were estimated to be between 120 and 130 mph.

A strong upper level storm system approached the plains from the southwest during the day on February 3rd, 2014.  By late evening, snow began to fall over extreme South Central Kansas.  After midnight on February 4th, 2014, snow spread across South Central and Central Kansas, especially along a line from Hutchinson to Salina.   By daybreak on February 4th, 2014,  snow rates increased across South Central Kansas.  Snow persisted throughout the day and into the evening hours resulting in snowfall totals up to nearly a foot in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to nine inches of snow fell across the county.   Blowing and drifting snow made roadways impassible.  Numerous auto accidents and slide offs occurred along Interstate 70.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to ten inches fell across the county.   Numerous auto slide offs and accidents occurred along Interstate 70.   Blowing and drifting of the snow made county roads impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass dropped south across the plains on March 1st, with a mixture of freezing drizzle and sleet.  This led to hazardous driving conditions across Kansas on March the 1st and March the 2nd, 2014.  The wintry mixture of freezing drizzle and sleet eventually transitioned to all snow as a significant upper level trough moved across the Midwest.   Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches across the area, with some areas also receiving an inch of sleet.  The heaviest snowfall occurred along US Highway 50 from just south of Hutchinson to Newton to near Mount Hope, where almost 5 inches of snow fell.  Numerous auto accidents occurred from the mixture of freezing drizzle, sleet and snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of snow fell across the area.  Mid Continent Airport in Wichita picked up 2.6 inches of snow and a half an inch of sleet.   One to two inches of sleet feel across the northern half of the county.  Numerous auto accidents and slide offs occurred with the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very windy conditions prevailed across portions of central Kansas producing several areas of downed tree limbs and power lines.  The winds generally were sustained around 40 mph for a couple of hours and the gusts reached into the upper 40 to middle 50 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 51 mph were also noted.   Power lines were blown down and blocked a few lanes of traffic.  Over 7000 people were without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward advancing warm front moved into portions of Southern Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours of April 2nd, 2014.   As this boundary moved north, showers and thunderstorms developed along it or to the south of it, generally south of US highway 54/400.  The storms developed into supercell thunderstorms, which produced large hail as large as two inches hit much of Allen County, especially over the city of Iola.   In South Central Kansas 2 inch hail impacted the Kansas Star Casino near Mulvane.  A brief tornado touchdown also occurred 5 miles southwest of Elk City in Chautauqua County, Kansas.||Multiple storms trained over Allen county, producing 3.77 inches of rain at the Iola Water Plant.  This abundance of rain caused county flooding and even some river flooding along the Neosho River.

An unusually dry winter and spring, pushed portions of Central and South Central Kansas, back into severe drought (D2) for April.  The very dry conditions continued during the month of April, with some areas falling 2 to 3 inches below normal for the month.  Coupled with a dry March, some areas of South Central Kansas, actually climbed into the extreme drought (D3) category by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unusually dry month of April, with precipitation amounts almost 2 inches below normal, led to most of the county moving into the severe drought (D2) category. Salina, Kansas airport was 1.72 inches below normal for April.  This deficit occurred  after being 2.09 inches below normal for March.

A strong upper level storm system approached the plains from the southwest during the day on February 3rd, 2014.  By late evening, snow began to fall over extreme South Central Kansas.  After midnight on February 4th, 2014, snow spread across South Central and Central Kansas, especially along a line from Hutchinson to Salina.   By daybreak on February 4th, 2014,  snow rates increased across South Central Kansas.  Snow persisted throughout the day and into the evening hours resulting in snowfall totals up to nearly a foot in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell across the county, with the higher amounts across the southeast part of the county.   Numerous auto slide offs were reported along Interstate 70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system approached the plains from the southwest during the day on February 3rd, 2014.  By late evening, snow began to fall over extreme South Central Kansas.  After midnight on February 4th, 2014, snow spread across South Central and Central Kansas, especially along a line from Hutchinson to Salina.   By daybreak on February 4th, 2014,  snow rates increased across South Central Kansas.  Snow persisted throughout the day and into the evening hours resulting in snowfall totals up to nearly a foot in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches to near a foot of snow fell across the county.  The intersection of Interstate 70 and Interstate 135 was closed for a short time due to a jack knifed semi tractor trailer. Numerous other accidents and slide offs were reported across the area including the city of Salina.  Blowing and drifting of the snow made side roads impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting during the late evening hours of Saturday the 22nd, peaking in intensity overnight and gradually moving out by late morning on the 23rd, a notably-narrow band of heavy snow in the 6-8 inch range targeted mainly the northern halves of Phillips and Smith counties, with isolated 6 amounts also reported as far east as central Jewell County.  Meanwhile, southern portions of these counties, along with all of Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties observed little to no accumulation. Within this six-county portion of North Central Kansas, the primary heavy snow band generally extended no more than 5-10 miles either side of a line stretched from near Long Island to Mankato. Per NWS cooperative observers, a few of the highest official snow totals featured 8.9 at Lebanon, 8.0 at Smith Center and 6.5 at Mankato. Only a short distance south of the primary band, locations such as Logan and Beloit hardly saw a single snowflake. Fortunately, winds were not a major issue during this event, with sustained northeasterly speeds of generally 10-15 MPH keeping blowing and drifting to a minimum. ||Timing-wise, this quasi-stationary heavy snow band first started building southward into extreme North Central Kansas out of southern Nebraska during the 10 PM-12 AM time frame, peaked in intensity during the pre-dawn hours, and gradually weakened and slid off to the east during the morning of the 23rd. Not surprisingly, the meteorological culprit for this narrow, banded snow event was a concentrated zone of mid level frontogenesis evident at the 700 millibar level, driven by lift from passing upper level jet streaks. On the larger scale aloft, a broad, low-amplitude trough encompassed most of the nation. At the surface during this snow event, North Central Kansas became increasingly under the influence of high pressure building southward out of southern Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 6.5 inches occurred in Mankato. However, lesser amounts occurred over a larger part of Jewell County, with 5.1 inches in Ionia and 3.8 inches in Burr Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, February 2014 marked the 21st consecutive month during which drought of at least category D2 Severe intensity plagued at least some portion of this six-county North Central Kansas area, prolonging one of the most notable droughts in decades. Unlike the preceding few months which featured widespread coverage of below normal precipitation, February was closer to being a 50/50 split between at least slightly above and below normal totals, according to reports from around 20 NWS cooperative observers and based on long-term February normals generally ranging between 0.50 and 0.75. As a result, and despite the fairly dry meteorological winter across most of the area, the weekly U.S. Drought Monitor reflected almost no change whatsoever in drought classification during February within the six county local area. Therefore, category D2 Severe Drought remained intact across essentially all of Phillips, Rooks, Smith and Osborne counties along with the western fringes of Jewell and Mitchell counties, while the majority of Jewell and Mitchell counties remained in D1 Moderate Drought.||The majority of cooperative observer stations within the local North Central Kansas area reported February precipitation totals somewhere between 0.50-0.80���, with most  of this precipitation falling during one or both of the month's two most significant snow events, one that occurred primarily on the 4th and the other during the early morning hours of the 23rd. As for monthly precipitation extremes, a few of the lowest tallies included 0.26 at Kirwin Dam, 0.31 at Logan and 0.49 at Phillipsburg. On the opposite side of the ledger were locations four miles west-northwest of Plainville (1.07), at Covert rural station in Osborne County (1.06) and in Lebanon (0.96). According to the USDA Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report at the end of February, state-averaged topsoil moisture supplies remained relatively unchanged since late-January, checking in 55% short to very short and only 44% adequate. Subsoil moisture supplies also remained similar, rating 61% short to very short and 39% adequate.

A compact winter weather system moved across portions of the plains during the late evening hours of February 9th and the early morning hours of February 10th, 2014. As this system moved across, portions of Central and South Central  Kansas received 2 to 5 inches of snow. The heaviest snowfall occurred along a swath from Great Bend, Kansas to Wichita to Chanute, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to five inches of snow fell across the county.  The city of Hutchinson received 2.5 inches of snow, while a COOP observer in Pretty Prairie received 4.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of May 7 a severe left moving thunderstorm developed in a very unstable and warm environment over central KS. As the storm moved northeast it produced up to hen egg sized hail and 65 mph winds. The combination of large hail and winds did cause damage to homes and vehicles.

After midnight on February 4th, 2014, snow spread across South Central and Central Kansas, especially along a line from Hutchinson to Salina.   By daybreak on February 4th, 2014,  snow rates increased across South Central Kansas.  Snow persisted throughout the day and into the evening hours resulting in snowfall totals up to nearly a foot in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine to twelve inches of snow fell across the county, with the highest amounts over the northwest portion of the county.  The city of Wichita, recorded 8.7 inches of snow.  Over a hundred auto accidents were reported in the city of Wichita, with 12 injuries. The high rate of the snow and the extreme cold that dropped temperatures into the single digits, hard packed the snow onto roads, which kept most city streets very slick for an additional 2 to 3 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms unleashed 60-70 mph gusts across the region as they dissipated late in the evening. Damage resulted, the most significant was that of a tractor trailer that was overturned on I-70 11 miles east of the I-135 interchange at Mile Post 261. The strong winds also downed power lines and knocked down power poles. Scattered tree damage also occurred, perhaps the most significant of which occurred in Belle Plaine where a large tree that was uprooted blocked a road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 60 knot gust was measured 4 miles north of Wellington. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening of June 14th and early morning hours of the 15th strong thunderstorms formed in western KS and southern NE. The storms eventually formed a line that produced winds up to 80 mph. The environment ahead of the line remained unstable and a strong low level jet contributed to the damaging winds. The line of thunderstorms gradually weakened as it headed eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs up to 8 inches in diameter down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A northward advancing warm front moved into portions of Southern Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours of April 2nd, 2014.   As this boundary moved north, showers and thunderstorms developed along it or to the south of it, generally south of US highway 54/400.  The storms developed into supercell thunderstorms, which produced large hail as large as two inches hit much of Allen County, especially over the city of Iola.   In South Central Kansas 2 inch hail impacted the Kansas Star Casino near Mulvane.  A brief tornado touchdown also occurred 5 miles southwest of Elk City in Chautauqua County, Kansas.||Multiple storms trained over Allen county, producing 3.77 inches of rain at the Iola Water Plant.  This abundance of rain caused county flooding and even some river flooding along the Neosho River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed across North Central Kansas on this Sunday afternoon and evening. The first storm formed over Osborne county around 3:30 p.m. CDT. Other storms quickly developed and joined it, with most of the region experiencing thunderstorms. Some of these storms became severe over portions of Jewell, Mitchell, Osborne, and Smith counties. The largest reported hail was the size of half dollars at Natoma and Burr Oak. Wind gusts were estimated at 60 MPH in the town of Osborne. ||A very long front extended from New England, through the Ohio Valley, across the Central Plains, and into the Desert Southwest. Portions of this front were stationary while others were being modulated by several waves of low pressure. The front lifted northward to the Kansas-Nebraska border by daybreak. One low ejected out of Colorado and moved east along the front through northern Kansas during the day. As this low progressed into northwestern Kansas, the dryline mixed eastward along with it, with a triple point forming at the location of the low. As the low continued moving east, the front dropped south and merged with the dryline. These storms formed along and head of the low and combined dryline/front. The upper level flow featured an amplified western trough and eastern ridge. A closed low was at the base of the trough over the Four Corners region. These storms initiated with the aid of a shortwave trough that ejected into the Central Plains ahead of this low. A strong 100 knot jet streak was at the base of the trough at 300 mb, placing the Central Plains in the favorable left front quadrant. Temperatures near the  time of thunderstorm initiation were near 90 degrees with dewpoints in the 60s. Combined with steep mid-level lapse rates of 8 degrees C/km, MLCAPE values were between 2000 and 3000 J/kg. Winds were strong throughout the column. Deep layer shear was in excess of 50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 MPH was accompanied by nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line segment of thunderstorms formed over central Kansas during the evening hours on this Wednesday evening. As the storms moved northeastward, new thunderstorms formed on its northern end and the northernmost storm moved into southeastern Mitchell county around 9 p.m. CDT. On its way through the eastern fringe of the county, the storm produced 2 reports of 1 inch hail.||These storms formed along a dryline that extended southward across central Kansas. In the upper levels, a deep trough was over the western U.S. with a ridge over the east. MLCAPE was between 1000 and 1500 J/kg with deep layer shear near 35 knots, creating a favorable environment for thunderstorm development.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms produced large hail, strong winds, very heavy rains & one tornado over South-Central & Southeast Kansas. The thunderstorms began in the afternoon of the 22nd & continued until late at night on the 24th. Although the thunderstorms produced large hail early in the event, the greatest hazard of all was extensive & prolonged flooding as the thunderstorms became prolific heavy rain-producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line segment of thunderstorms formed over central Kansas during the evening hours on this Wednesday evening. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak thunderstorm moved across the city of Topeka on the evening of June 29th. At approximately 920 pm CDT a brief tornado touched down and skipped along a 1.6 mile path causing tree limb damage in 3 locations.  The tornado was never photographed although several photos and videos appeared to show a lowering beneath a bowl shaped updraft base.  The tornado was rated an EF0 with winds perhaps up to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Only report received was from the public who called 911 and was transferred to the NWS at 923pm CDT.  Report was of a possible tornado with some debris lofted.  Unfortunately no other reports were received by the NWS via calls or on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small bow echo tracked through North Central Kansas after the midnight hour on this Thursday. The bow tracked across northwestern Kansas during the mid to late evening hours, but it weakened as it progressed into Phillips and Rooks counties around midnight. However, around 1:00 a.m. CDT, the short line segment of storms strengthened as it entered Osborne and Smith counties. Other scattered storms also began developing along Interstate 70, with northeastward movement toward North Central Kansas. There was a single report of 1 inch hail over extreme southern Osborne county. Radar did suggest that a few other spots might have received severe hail, but the time of the night and the lack of population precluded receiving additional reports. The mesonet stations affected by these storms only measured wind gusts to near 40 MPH.||These storms originally formed over Colorado due to easterly upslope flow, north of a stationary front that extended across central and southern Kansas. This did create a pattern of warm air advection and frontogenesis, since the thermal ridge was over central Kansas. In the upper levels, a warm subtropical high was over the Gulf Coast and a cut-off low was over Nevada and California. A ridge extended northwest, between between these two features, over the Central Plains. Southwest flow in the mid-levels provided steep lapse rates, resulting in MUCAPE of 1000-2000 J/kg. Effective shear was 40 to 50 knots.

The thunderstorms developed in an unstable atmosphere ahead of a dry line and surface trough as it moved along the Kansas Turnpike.  The supercell thunderstorm that moved along Highway 54/400 was a prolific hail producer with some of the hail as large as golf balls.  The supercell also produced a brief tornado touchdown near Severy, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The trained spotter reported pea to penny sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface cyclone situated over the Western Plains produced 45-60 mph winds across parts of Central Kansas during the noon hour. Barton, McPherson & Russell counties were hit the hardest where gusts around 60 mph occurred. A High Wind Warning had been issued at 1250 PM CDT & had been in effect until 700 PM CDT. The High Wind Warning was cancelled at 504 PM CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds across the county were estimated to be 40 mph with gusts in the 50s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms unleashed 60-70 mph gusts across the region as they dissipated late in the evening. Damage resulted, the most significant was that of a tractor trailer that was overturned on I-70 11 miles east of the I-135 interchange at Mile Post 261. The strong winds also downed power lines and knocked down power poles. Scattered tree damage also occurred, perhaps the most significant of which occurred in Belle Plaine where a large tree that was uprooted blocked a road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dissipating thunderstorms produced a 59-mph gust that was measured at Mid-Continent Airport. Also, the strong winds caused downed power lines and power poles 2 miles west of Derby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening of June 14th and early morning hours of the 15th strong thunderstorms formed in western KS and southern NE. The storms eventually formed a line that produced winds up to 80 mph. The environment ahead of the line remained unstable and a strong low level jet contributed to the damaging winds. The line of thunderstorms gradually weakened as it headed eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted. There was damage to the high school roof. Numerous power lines down over a large swath of Wellsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated supercell thunderstorms moved across portions of South Central Kansas on the evening of May 10th, 2014.  The thunderstorms developed in an unstable atmosphere ahead of a dry line and surface trough as it moved along the Kansas Turnpike.  The supercell thunderstorm that moved along Highway 54/400 was a prolific hail producer with some of the hail as large as golf balls.  The supercell also produced a brief tornado touchdown near Severy, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The large hail ranged in size from half dollar to golf ball size.  The hail was driven by 70 mph winds, causing damage to cars and house siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although this Wednesday afternoon and especially evening featured the first truly widespread thunderstorm event of the spring season in this six-county portion of North Central Kansas, fortunately the vast majority of severe weather focused slightly to the north in Nebraska and also slightly to the west in northwest Kansas. In fact, the only severe-criteria report from the local area was a 58 MPH wind gust at the Phillipsburg airport at 814 pm CDT as strong outflow winds surged into the area along a line of thunderstorms marching in from the west. On a positive note, much of North Central Kansas received much-needed modest rainfall totals between 0.50-1.00 from this event, with higher totals of generally 1.50-2.50 centered over Phillips County including 2.48 reported by the NWS cooperative observer in Phillipsburg. ||As for event-timing, a smattering of isolated convection initially flared up within the local area between 330-500 pm CDT. However, for the next few hours North Central Kansas remained storm-free as the vast majority of convection focused off to the north and west within a narrow corridor along an invading cold front. Eventually, several rounds of mainly sub-severe multicell storms pushed into the Phillips, Rooks and Smith counties between 7-11 pm CDT, with much of this activity linear in nature. As this activity steadily weakened toward the midnight hour due to the onset of diurnal cooling and the increasingly outflow-dominant mode, it also expanded in coverage across the remainder of the local area, with light to moderate rain lingering well into the early morning hours of the 24th. In the mid-upper levels, the primary forcing for this event was provided by low-amplitude shortwave troughs kicking out across the Central Plains in southwesterly flow, downstream from a large-scale trough axis slowly sliding east through the Rockies. At the surface, the aforementioned, well-defined cold front was the primary low-level trigger, as this front stretched from northeast Nebraska through northwest Kansas during the late afternoon where it connected to a strong 993 millibar low centered in far eastern Colorado. Although low-level moisture was seasonably sparse with surface dewpoints only averaging in the upper 40s-low 50s F, steep low-mid level lapse rates promoted modest mixed-layer CAPE of 500-1000 J/kg in the presence of respectable 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 40-50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This thunderstorm wind gust was measured coming from the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions early in the month led to the drought continuing across the area for the first week of June.  Welcome rainfall began to occur during the middle part of the month, and continued for the rest of the month, bringing drought relief to much of the region.  The heavy rainfall continued for much of the month, reducing or ending the drought in some locations.  The dry spring was too late to help the wheat crop, but hindered the wheat harvest, as the wet fields late in the month made for a slow harvest.  Eight to 10 inch rainfall amounts were common, with Wichita, Kansas reporting the third wettest June on record, with 10.46 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry start to the month of June, led to most of the county remaining in the severe drought (D2) category for the first week of June. Welcome rainfall fell in the middle of the month and continued until the end of the month, bringing much needed relief to drought stricken areas.  This led to the drought category dropping to moderate (D1) by the end of the month.  Rainfall amounts by the end of the month actually climbed well above normal for the first time in calendar year 2014.

Damaging wind gusts to 70 mph also occurred with the storms. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of severe occurred during the evening of May, 7th, 2014. The supercell thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours of May 7th and tracked quickly to the north. These storms intensified as they tracked north producing large hail and damaging straight-line winds.  The town of Galva, Kansas, was hit hard shortly after 6 pm by the storms with numerous power poles snapped along with wind blown hail causing roof and siding damage to several homes. In addition, straight-line winds in excess of 70 mph derailed 30 railroad cars between Galva and Canton, Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually dry winter and spring, pushed portions of Central and South Central Kansas, back into extreme (D3) and severe drought (D2) for May 2104. The very dry conditions continued during the month of May, with some areas falling another half an inch to an inch below normal for rainfall, increasing the rainfall deficit for the year to over 4 inches below normal. Coupled with a dry March and April, the extreme drought (D3) increased across South Central Kansas.  Some welcome rainfall did begin to the return to portions of Central and South Central Kansas toward the end of the month that brought some relief from the drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another unusually dry month of May, with precipitation amounts almost 1 inch below normal, led to most of the county remaining in the severe drought (D2) category. Salina, Kansas airport was 0.80 inches below normal for May. This deficit occurred after being 1.72 inches below normal for April.  Some welcome rainfall fell late in the month, bringing some relief to drought stricken areas, but too late to help the winter wheat crop which was rated poor to well below average.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms began to develop during the mid afternoon along a dry line over Western Kansas.  The storms moved northeast over a cold front that remained stationary roughly along Highway 24, before moving east across the area.  The largest hail of 1.5 inches was reported southwest of Oakley, and the strongest thunderstorm wind gusts reported were 60 MPH over Norton county.  The thunderstorms trained over Sheridan, Graham, Norton, and parts of Decatur counties during the evening before the cold front pushed the storm activity east of Norton and Graham counties after midnight.  Rainfall totals ranged from 2.29 inches 3 S of Lenora, to 2.8 inches 4 NNE of Studley and 1.78 inches near Morland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail lasted for ten minutes. Heavy rain occurred with minor street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of severe occurred during the evening of May, 7th, 2014. The supercell thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours of May 7th and tracked quickly to the north. These storms intensified as they tracked north producing large hail and damaging straight-line winds.  The town of Galva, Kansas, was hit hard shortly after 6 pm by the storms with numerous power poles snapped along with wind blown hail causing roof and siding damage to several homes. In addition, straight-line winds in excess of 70 mph derailed 30 railroad cars between Galva and Canton, Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms began to develop during the mid afternoon along a dry line over Western Kansas.  The storms moved northeast over a cold front that remained stationary roughly along Highway 24, before moving east across the area.  The largest hail of 1.5 inches was reported southwest of Oakley, and the strongest thunderstorm wind gusts reported were 60 MPH over Norton county.  The thunderstorms trained over Sheridan, Graham, Norton, and parts of Decatur counties during the evening before the cold front pushed the storm activity east of Norton and Graham counties after midnight.  Rainfall totals ranged from 2.29 inches 3 S of Lenora, to 2.8 inches 4 NNE of Studley and 1.78 inches near Morland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail started covering the ground, then the size of the hail dropped to dime size with heavy rain developing.

On the afternoon of May 10 a very unstable air mass and shear was in place as thunderstorms developed along a surface trough moving through eastern KS. The storms split and the left movers became dominate. The most impactful storm moved northeast along the KS turnpike and into the south side of Topeka. It produced at least golf ball sized hail through most of it's life span. The large hail reported was baseball sized. Strong winds along with the hail caused damage to vehicles and homes.

A large and powerful complex of thunderstorms moved southeast out of northwest Kansas and across the area during the early morning hours of June 5th, 2014.  This complex of storms was a prolific producer of very large hail up to three and half inches in diameter, especially considering the early morning hours when it occurred.  Damaging wind gusts to 70 mph also occurred with the storms.  As the complex moved southeast, a wake low developed on the north side of the system, producing non thunderstorm wind damage across portions of Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement estimated winds of 50 to 60 mph winds and dime sized hail.

An unusually dry winter and spring, pushed portions of Central and South Central Kansas, back into extreme (D3) and severe drought (D2) for May 2104. The very dry conditions continued during the month of May, with some areas falling another half an inch to an inch below normal for rainfall, increasing the rainfall deficit for the year to over 4 inches below normal. Coupled with a dry March and April, the extreme drought (D3) increased across South Central Kansas.  Some welcome rainfall did begin to the return to portions of Central and South Central Kansas toward the end of the month that brought some relief from the drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another unusually dry month of May, with precipitation amounts almost 1 inch below normal, led to most of the county remaining in the severe drought (D2) category. Some welcome rainfall fell late in the month, bringing some relief to drought stricken areas, but too late to help the winter wheat crop which was rated poor to well below average.

Northwest Kansas counties showed no change in terms of drought status during the month of May. The entire area remains in either D2 (Severe) or D3 (Extreme) Drought. Precipitation during the month was below normal at all reporting sites, with departures ranging from 0.25 to nearly 3.0 below average. The driest areas in May were Norton, Graham and Sheridan counties in the east, while observers in Thomas and Rawlins counties reported precipitation only 0.25 to 1.0 below average.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cheyenne County continued in D3 (Extreme) Drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions early in the month led to the drought continuing across the area for the first week of June.  Welcome rainfall began to occur during the middle part of the month, and continued for the rest of the month, bringing drought relief to much of the region.  The heavy rainfall continued for much of the month, reducing or ending the drought in some locations.  The dry spring was too late to help the wheat crop, but hindered the wheat harvest, as the wet fields late in the month made for a slow harvest.  Eight to 10 inch rainfall amounts were common, with Wichita, Kansas reporting the third wettest June on record, with 10.46 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry start to the month of June, led to most of the county remaining in the severe drought (D2) category for the first week of June. Welcome rainfall fell in the middle of the month and continued until the end of the month, bringing much needed relief to drought stricken areas.  This led to the drought category dropping to moderate (D1) by the end of the month.  Rainfall amounts by the end of the month actually climbed well above normal for the first time in calendar year 2014.  Chanute Airport recorded 8.05 inches, which was 2 inches above normal.

Strong to severe thunderstorms began to develop during the mid afternoon along a dry line over Western Kansas.  The storms moved northeast over a cold front that remained stationary roughly along Highway 24, before moving east across the area.  The largest hail of 1.5 inches was reported southwest of Oakley, and the strongest thunderstorm wind gusts reported were 60 MPH over Norton county.  The thunderstorms trained over Sheridan, Graham, Norton, and parts of Decatur counties during the evening before the cold front pushed the storm activity east of Norton and Graham counties after midnight.  Rainfall totals ranged from 2.29 inches 3 S of Lenora, to 2.8 inches 4 NNE of Studley and 1.78 inches near Morland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent heavy rainfall accompanied the hail.  Spotter received 0.80 of rain so far.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of severe occurred during the evening of May, 7th, 2014. The supercell thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours of May 7th and tracked quickly to the north. These storms intensified as they tracked north producing large hail and damaging straight-line winds.  The town of Galva, Kansas, was hit hard shortly after 6 pm by the storms with numerous power poles snapped along with wind blown hail causing roof and siding damage to several homes. In addition, straight-line winds in excess of 70 mph derailed 30 railroad cars between Galva and Canton, Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was reported from the public through KSN-TV media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and powerful complex of thunderstorms moved southeast out of northwest Kansas and across the area during the early morning hours of June 5th, 2014.  This complex of storms was a prolific producer of very large hail up to three and half inches in diameter, especially considering the early morning hours when it occurred.  Damaging wind gusts to 70 mph also occurred with the storms.  As the complex moved southeast, a wake low developed on the north side of the system, producing non thunderstorm wind damage across portions of Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tin shed was blown apart and wrapped around a power pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions early in the month led to the drought continuing across the area for the first week of June.  Welcome rainfall began to occur during the middle part of the month, and continued for the rest of the month, bringing drought relief to much of the region.  The heavy rainfall continued for much of the month, reducing or ending the drought in some locations.  The dry spring was too late to help the wheat crop, but hindered the wheat harvest, as the wet fields late in the month made for a slow harvest.  Eight to 10 inch rainfall amounts were common, with Wichita, Kansas reporting the third wettest June on record, with 10.46 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dry start to the month of June, led to most of the county remaining in the extreme drought (D3) category for the first week of June. Welcome rainfall fell in the middle of the month and continued until the end of the month, bringing much needed relief to drought stricken areas.  This led to the drought category dropping to moderate (D1) by the end of the month.  Rainfall amounts by the end of the month actually climbed well above normal for the first time in calendar year 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually dry winter and spring, pushed portions of Central and South Central Kansas, back into extreme (D3) and severe drought (D2) for May 2104. The very dry conditions continued during the month of May, with some areas falling another half an inch to an inch below normal for rainfall, increasing the rainfall deficit for the year to over 4 inches below normal. Coupled with a dry March and April, the extreme drought (D3) increased across South Central Kansas.  Some welcome rainfall did begin to the return to portions of Central and South Central Kansas toward the end of the month that brought some relief from the drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another unusually dry month of May, with precipitation amounts almost 1 inch below normal, led to most of the county climbing into the extreme drought (D3) category. Some welcome rainfall fell late in the month, bringing some relief to drought stricken areas, but too late to help the winter wheat crop which was rated poor to well below average.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening of June 14th and early morning hours of the 15th strong thunderstorms formed in western KS and southern NE. The storms eventually formed a line that produced winds up to 80 mph. The environment ahead of the line remained unstable and a strong low level jet contributed to the damaging winds. The line of thunderstorms gradually weakened as it headed eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated 60 to 70 mph.
